title: Conditional Input Demand for a Two-Output Technology
learning_objective: Derive conditional input demand from a binding production requirement.
preliminaries: Input prices are \(w_1,w_2>0\). Outputs are \(q=(q_1,q_2)\in\mathbb{R}_+^2\). Producing \(q\) requires inputs \(z=(z_1,z_2)\in\mathbb{R}_+^2\) satisfying \(z_1^{1/3}z_2^{1/3}\ge (q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{1/\sigma}\), where \(\sigma>0\).
question: Derive the conditional input demand \(z(w,q)\).
solution: Let \(A(q)=(q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{1/\sigma}\). The cost-minimization problem is \[\min_{z_1,z_2\ge 0} w_1z_1+w_2z_2 \quad \text{s.t.}\quad z_1^{1/3}z_2^{1/3}\ge A(q).\] For \(q\ne 0\), the constraint binds and the optimum is interior. Using logs, the constraint is \((1/3)\log z_1+(1/3)\log z_2\ge \log A(q)\). The FOCs are \(w_1=\lambda/(3z_1)\) and \(w_2=\lambda/(3z_2)\), so \(w_1z_1=w_2z_2\) and \(z_1=(w_2/w_1)z_2\). The binding constraint implies \(z_1z_2=A(q)^3=(q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{3/\sigma}\). Therefore \[z_1(w,q)=\sqrt{\frac{w_2}{w_1}}(q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{3/(2\sigma)},\qquad z_2(w,q)=\sqrt{\frac{w_1}{w_2}}(q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{3/(2\sigma)}.\] If \(q=(0,0)\), the same formula gives \(z=(0,0)\).

title: Cost Function for a Multi-Output Requirement
learning_objective: Compute the cost function by substituting conditional input demands.
preliminaries: Use the technology \(z_1^{1/3}z_2^{1/3}\ge (q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{1/\sigma}\) and input prices \(w_1,w_2>0\). The conditional input demands are \(z_1=\sqrt{w_2/w_1}(q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{3/(2\sigma)}\) and \(z_2=\sqrt{w_1/w_2}(q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{3/(2\sigma)}\).
question: Derive the cost function \(C(w,q)\).
solution: The cost function is the minimized value \(C(w,q)=w_1z_1(w,q)+w_2z_2(w,q)\). Substitution gives \[C(w,q)=w_1\sqrt{\frac{w_2}{w_1}}(q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{3/(2\sigma)}+w_2\sqrt{\frac{w_1}{w_2}}(q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{3/(2\sigma)}.\] Both terms equal \(\sqrt{w_1w_2}(q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{3/(2\sigma)}\), so \[C(w,q)=2\sqrt{w_1w_2}(q_1^\sigma+q_2^\sigma)^{3/(2\sigma)}.\] This also gives zero cost at \(q=(0,0)\), as expected.

title: Output Supply with \(\sigma=3/2\)
learning_objective: Use a cost function to derive profit-maximizing outputs.
preliminaries: Let output prices be \(p_1,p_2>0\), input prices be \(w_1,w_2>0\), and set \(\sigma=3/2\). The cost function is \(C(w,q)=2\sqrt{w_1w_2}(q_1^{3/2}+q_2^{3/2})\).
question: Derive the profit-maximizing output vector \(q^*(p,w)\).
solution: The firm solves \[\max_{q_1,q_2\ge 0} p_1q_1+p_2q_2-2\sqrt{w_1w_2}(q_1^{3/2}+q_2^{3/2}).\] The objective is strictly concave in \((q_1,q_2)\) because \(q_i^{3/2}\) is strictly convex and enters with a negative coefficient. Since \(p_i>0\), the optimum is interior. The FOCs are \[p_i-3\sqrt{w_1w_2}q_i^{1/2}=0\quad i=1,2.\] Hence \[q_i^*(p,w)=\frac{p_i^2}{9w_1w_2}\quad i=1,2.\]

title: Unconditional Input Demand with \(\sigma=3/2\)
learning_objective: Obtain unconditional input demand by composing conditional demand with output supply.
preliminaries: For \(\sigma=3/2\), conditional input demands are \(z_1(w,q)=\sqrt{w_2/w_1}(q_1^{3/2}+q_2^{3/2})\) and \(z_2(w,q)=\sqrt{w_1/w_2}(q_1^{3/2}+q_2^{3/2})\). Profit-maximizing outputs are \(q_i^*(p,w)=p_i^2/(9w_1w_2)\).
question: Derive the unconditional input demands \(x_1^*(p,w)\) and \(x_2^*(p,w)\).
solution: Unconditional input demand is conditional demand evaluated at the profit-maximizing output: \(x^*(p,w)=z(w,q^*(p,w))\). Since \((q_i^*)^{3/2}=p_i^3/(27(w_1w_2)^{3/2})\), \[q_1^{*3/2}+q_2^{*3/2}=\frac{p_1^3+p_2^3}{27(w_1w_2)^{3/2}}.\] Therefore \[x_1^*(p,w)=\sqrt{\frac{w_2}{w_1}}\frac{p_1^3+p_2^3}{27(w_1w_2)^{3/2}}=\frac{p_1^3+p_2^3}{27w_1^2w_2},\] and \[x_2^*(p,w)=\sqrt{\frac{w_1}{w_2}}\frac{p_1^3+p_2^3}{27(w_1w_2)^{3/2}}=\frac{p_1^3+p_2^3}{27w_1w_2^2}.\]

title: Input Price Derivative of Profit
learning_objective: Apply Hotelling's lemma to an input price derivative.
preliminaries: For a differentiable profit function \(\pi(p,w)=\max_q\{p\cdot q-C(w,q)\}\), Hotelling's lemma gives \(\partial \pi/\partial w_i=-x_i^*(p,w)\), where \(x_i^*\) is unconditional demand for input \(i\). In this problem \(x_1^*(p,w)=(p_1^3+p_2^3)/(27w_1^2w_2)\).
question: Give \(\partial \pi(p,w)/\partial w_1\) without first deriving and differentiating a closed-form profit function.
solution: By Hotelling's lemma, the derivative of maximum profit with respect to an input price equals minus the profit-maximizing use of that input. Therefore \[\frac{\partial \pi(p,w)}{\partial w_1}=-x_1^*(p,w)=-\frac{p_1^3+p_2^3}{27w_1^2w_2}.\] The sign is negative because increasing an input price lowers profit at the rate at which the firm uses that input, holding the optimized choice fixed by the envelope theorem.

title: The Work-Minimization Problem
learning_objective: Formulate a dual work-minimization problem with labor entering preferences.
preliminaries: A consumer chooses \(x\in\mathbb{R}_+^L\) and work hours \(h\ge 0\). Utility \(u(x,h)\) is continuous, strictly increasing in each component of \(x\), and weakly decreasing in \(h\). The wage is normalized to one, so the budget constraint is \(p\cdot x\le h\).
question: For target utility \(\bar u\), write the work-minimization problem and define the minimal-work function.
solution: The work-minimization problem is \[m(p,\bar u)=\inf_{x\ge 0,h\ge 0}\{h:u(x,h)\ge \bar u,\ p\cdot x\le h\}.\] The value \(m(p,\bar u)\) is the minimal number of work hours needed to afford a bundle that attains utility at least \(\bar u\). Since utility is weakly decreasing in \(h\), any feasible pair with \(h>p\cdot x\) can reduce \(h\) down to \(p\cdot x\) without hurting feasibility or lowering utility. Thus an equivalent reduced problem is \[m(p,\bar u)=\inf_{x\ge 0}\{p\cdot x:u(x,p\cdot x)\ge \bar u\}.\]

title: Minimal Work Is Nondecreasing in Prices
learning_objective: Prove price monotonicity of the minimal-work function by set inclusion.
preliminaries: The minimal-work function is \(m(p,\bar u)=\inf\{h:u(x,h)\ge \bar u,\ p\cdot x\le h,\ x\ge 0,h\ge 0\}\). Utility is weakly decreasing in \(h\) and strictly increasing in goods.
question: Prove that \(m(p,\bar u)\) is nondecreasing in each component of \(p\).
solution: Take price vectors \(p'\ge p\) componentwise. If \((x,h)\) is feasible at prices \(p'\), then \(u(x,h)\ge \bar u\) and \(p'\cdot x\le h\). Since \(p\le p'\), we also have \(p\cdot x\le p'\cdot x\le h\). Thus every pair feasible at \(p'\) is feasible at \(p\). The feasible set at lower prices contains the feasible set at higher prices. Minimizing the same objective \(h\) over the larger feasible set cannot yield a larger value, so \(m(p,\bar u)\le m(p',\bar u)\). Therefore raising any goods price weakly increases minimal work.

title: A Counterexample to Homogeneity of Minimal Work
learning_objective: Show that minimal work need not be homogeneous of degree one when work enters utility.
preliminaries: Consider one consumption good with price \(p>0\). Work hours are \(h\ge 0\), the budget constraint is \(px\le h\), and the target utility is \(\bar u=1\). Utility is \(u(x,h)=x^2/h\) for \(h>0\).
question: Use this example to show that the minimal-work function need not be homogeneous of degree one in \(p\).
solution: At an optimum, \(h=px\); otherwise the consumer could reduce \(h\), which raises \(x^2/h\) and lowers the objective. The utility constraint becomes \[\frac{x^2}{px}=\frac{x}{p}\ge 1,\] so \(x\ge p\). Minimal work is therefore \[m(p,1)=\min_{x\ge p}px=p^2.\] If prices double, \(m(2p,1)=4p^2\), while degree-one homogeneity would require \(m(2p,1)=2m(p,1)=2p^2\). Thus the minimal-work function is not generally homogeneous of degree one in prices.

title: Homogeneity When Utility Is Independent of Work
learning_objective: Recover expenditure-function homogeneity when work hours do not affect utility.
preliminaries: Suppose utility is independent of work hours, so \(u(x,h)=v(x)\). The minimal-work function is \(m(p,\bar u)=\inf\{h:v(x)\ge \bar u,\ p\cdot x\le h,\ x\ge 0,h\ge 0\}\).
question: Show that \(m(p,\bar u)\) is homogeneous of degree one in \(p\) when utility is constant in \(h\).
solution: If \(u(x,h)=v(x)\), the minimal-work problem reduces to \[m(p,\bar u)=\inf_{x\ge 0}\{p\cdot x:v(x)\ge \bar u\}.\] This is the standard expenditure problem, expressed in work-hours units because the wage is normalized to one. For any \(\lambda>0\), \[m(\lambda p,\bar u)=\inf_{x:v(x)\ge \bar u}\lambda p\cdot x=\lambda\inf_{x:v(x)\ge \bar u}p\cdot x=\lambda m(p,\bar u).\] Therefore the minimal-work function is homogeneous of degree one in prices under this additional assumption.

title: Monopoly Pricing with Shutdown Demand
learning_objective: Solve monopoly pricing with a boundary case where marginal cost exceeds choke price.
preliminaries: A monopolist has constant marginal cost \(c_1>0\) and faces demand \(D(p)=\max\{1-p,0\}\). Profit is \(\pi(p)=(p-c_1)D(p)\).
question: Find the monopoly price as \(c_1\) varies.
solution: If \(c_1\ge 1\), any price below one has negative margin and any price at least one yields zero demand and zero profit. Thus setting \(p=1\) is optimal. If \(0<c_1<1\), the firm maximizes \((p-c_1)(1-p)\) on \([c_1,1]\). The objective is strictly concave, and the FOC is \(1+c_1-2p=0\). Hence \(p^M=(1+c_1)/2\). Combining cases, \[p^M(c_1)=\begin{cases}(1+c_1)/2,&0<c_1\le 1,\\1,&c_1>1.\end{cases}\]

title: Equal Prices in Pure Bertrand Equilibrium
learning_objective: Prove that pure equilibria in this asymmetric-cost Bertrand game must have equal prices.
preliminaries: Two firms sell a homogeneous good. Costs satisfy \(0<c_1<c_2\). Demand is \(D(p)=\max\{1-p,0\}\). Consumers buy from the lower-price firm; if prices tie, firm 1 receives share \(\alpha_1\in[0,1]\). Firm 1's monopoly price is \(p_1^M\), and \(c_2<p_1^M\).
question: Show that any pure-strategy Nash equilibrium must have \(p_1=p_2\).
solution: Suppose first that \(p_1>p_2\). If \(p_2\ge c_2\), firm 1 gets no demand and can undercut to a price in \((c_1,p_2)\), earning positive profit. If \(p_2<c_2\), firm 2 earns negative profit when it sells and can raise to \(c_2\) to get nonnegative profit. Thus \(p_1>p_2\) is impossible. Suppose instead that \(p_1<p_2\). If \(p_1\ge c_2\), firm 2 can undercut firm 1 slightly and earn positive profit. If \(p_1<c_2\), firm 1 can raise price slightly while staying below \(\min\{p_2,c_2\}\), keeping all demand and increasing its margin. Thus \(p_1<p_2\) is impossible. Therefore any pure equilibrium must have equal prices.

title: Bertrand Equilibrium with Favorable Tie-Breaking
learning_objective: Verify the undominated pure equilibrium when ties favor the low-cost firm.
preliminaries: In the asymmetric-cost Bertrand game, \(0<c_1<c_2<p_1^M\), where \(p_1^M\) is firm 1's monopoly price. Demand is \(D(p)=\max\{1-p,0\}\). If prices tie and \(\alpha_1=1\), firm 1 serves the whole market.
question: Find the unique pure-strategy Nash equilibrium in weakly undominated strategies when \(\alpha_1=1\).
solution: The equilibrium is \((p_1,p_2)=(c_2,c_2)\). Firm 1 serves the whole market and earns \((c_2-c_1)(1-c_2)>0\). Because \(c_2<p_1^M\), firm 1's monopoly profit is increasing at \(c_2\), so lowering price reduces profit, while raising price loses demand. Firm 2 earns zero. It cannot profitably lower price below \(c_2\), because that gives negative margin, and raising price gives zero demand and the same zero profit. Thus \((c_2,c_2)\) is a Nash equilibrium. In weakly undominated strategies it is unique: prices below \(c_2\) are weakly dominated for firm 2, and any common price above \(c_2\) can be profitably undercut by firm 2. Therefore \((c_2,c_2)\) is the unique pure equilibrium in weakly undominated strategies.

title: No Pure Equilibrium with Unfavorable Tie-Breaking
learning_objective: Show why a pure equilibrium fails when the low-cost firm receives less than the full tie market.
preliminaries: Use the asymmetric-cost Bertrand model with \(0<c_1<c_2<p_1^M\). At equal prices, firm 1 receives market share \(\alpha_1\in[0,1)\). Any pure equilibrium must have equal prices.
question: Is there a pure-strategy Nash equilibrium when \(\alpha_1<1\)?
solution: No. Any pure equilibrium must have \(p_1=p_2=p\). If \(p<c_2\), firm 2 sells at negative margin or uses a weakly dominated below-cost price. If \(p>c_2\), firm 2 can slightly undercut and earn positive profit. If \(p=c_2\), firm 1 receives only share \(\alpha_1<1\) and earns \(\alpha_1(c_2-c_1)(1-c_2)\). By choosing \(c_2-\varepsilon\) for sufficiently small \(\varepsilon>0\), firm 1 captures the full market at almost the same margin and demand, earning strictly more because \(\alpha_1<1\). Thus no common price can be a Nash equilibrium, so no pure-strategy equilibrium exists.

title: Nonattainment in a Discontinuous Undercutting Problem
learning_objective: Explain why a best response may fail to exist because the profitable undercutting set is open.
preliminaries: Firm 1 has cost \(c_1\), firm 2 posts a finite price \(p_2>c_2>c_1\), and ties give firm 1 share \(\alpha_1<1\). Demand is \(D(p)=1-p\) for \(p\le 1\), and assume the relevant prices are below the choke price.
question: Does firm 1's profit maximization problem always have a solution against fixed \(p_2>c_2\) when \(\alpha_1<1\)?
solution: Not always. If firm 1 sets \(p_1<p_2\), it captures the market and earns \((p_1-c_1)(1-p_1)\). If it sets \(p_1=p_2\), it receives only share \(\alpha_1<1\). If the monopoly profit function is increasing up to \(p_2\), then firm 1 wants the highest price strictly below \(p_2\). The relevant problem is \[\max_{p_1\in[c_1,p_2)}(p_1-c_1)(1-p_1).\] This interval is open at \(p_2\). The supremum is approached as \(p_1\uparrow p_2\), but it is not attained. Pricing exactly \(p_2\) causes a discontinuous drop in demand share because \(\alpha_1<1\). Hence the best-response problem may have no solution.

title: Nash Equilibrium by Strict Dominance
learning_objective: Find the Nash equilibrium of a two-action simultaneous game.
preliminaries: Player 1 chooses row \(S\) or \(C\); Player 2 chooses column \(S\) or \(C\). Payoffs are \((S,S)=(5,2)\), \((S,C)=(3,1)\), \((C,S)=(6,3)\), and \((C,C)=(4,4)\), with Player 1's payoff first.
question: Find all Nash equilibria of the simultaneous-move game.
solution: For Player 1, \(C\) strictly dominates \(S\): against Player 2's \(S\), \(6>5\), and against Player 2's \(C\), \(4>3\). Thus Player 1 must choose \(C\) in any Nash equilibrium. Given \(C\), Player 2 compares \(S\), which gives \(3\), with \(C\), which gives \(4\). Player 2 chooses \(C\). Therefore the unique Nash equilibrium is \((C,C)\).

title: Stackelberg Equilibrium by Backward Induction
learning_objective: Solve the observed-action sequential version of a two-action game.
preliminaries: Use the payoff matrix \((S,S)=(5,2)\), \((S,C)=(3,1)\), \((C,S)=(6,3)\), and \((C,C)=(4,4)\). Player 1 moves first; Player 2 observes Player 1's action and then chooses \(S\) or \(C\).
question: Find the subgame-perfect equilibrium of the sequential game.
solution: Use backward induction. If Player 1 chooses \(S\), Player 2 gets \(2\) from \(S\) and \(1\) from \(C\), so Player 2 chooses \(S\). If Player 1 chooses \(C\), Player 2 gets \(3\) from \(S\) and \(4\) from \(C\), so Player 2 chooses \(C\). Anticipating this, Player 1 compares \(5\) from choosing \(S\) with \(4\) from choosing \(C\). Player 1 chooses \(S\). The unique subgame-perfect equilibrium is: Player 1 chooses \(S\); Player 2 chooses \(S\) after \(S\) and \(C\) after \(C\).

title: Why Always Playing \(S\) Is Not a PBE
learning_objective: Check a proposed PBE by testing the leader's deviation incentive.
preliminaries: Player 1 chooses \(S\) or \(C\). Player 2 observes a noisy signal and then chooses \(S\) or \(C\). Payoffs are \((S,S)=(5,2)\), \((S,C)=(3,1)\), \((C,S)=(6,3)\), and \((C,C)=(4,4)\). The proposed strategy profile is Player 1 chooses \(S\), and Player 2 chooses \(S\) after every signal.
question: For \(0<\varepsilon<1\), is the proposed strategy profile a Perfect Bayesian equilibrium?
solution: No. Given beliefs that put probability one on Player 1 having chosen \(S\), Player 2 is willing to choose \(S\) after either signal, because against \(S\), Player 2 gets \(2\) from \(S\) and \(1\) from \(C\). But Player 1's strategy is not sequentially optimal given Player 2's strategy. If Player 1 chooses \(S\), Player 2 chooses \(S\), and Player 1 gets \(5\). If Player 1 deviates to \(C\), Player 2 still chooses \(S\) after every signal, and Player 1 gets \(6\). Since \(6>5\), Player 1 has a profitable deviation. Therefore the proposed profile is not a PBE for any \(0<\varepsilon<1\).

title: Bayesian Beliefs in a Noisy-Leader Game
learning_objective: Compute posterior beliefs after noisy signals and derive the follower's indifference condition.
preliminaries: Player 1 chooses \(S\) with probability \(p\) and \(C\) with probability \(1-p\). The signal satisfies \(\Pr(s\mid S)=\Pr(c\mid C)=1-\varepsilon\). Player 2's payoffs are \(2\) from \((S,S)\), \(1\) from \((S,C)\), \(3\) from \((C,S)\), and \(4\) from \((C,C)\).
question: Compute \(\Pr(S\mid s)\) and \(\Pr(S\mid c)\). What value of \(p\) makes Player 2 indifferent after signal \(c\)?
solution: Bayes' rule gives \[\Pr(S\mid s)=\frac{(1-\varepsilon)p}{(1-\varepsilon)p+\varepsilon(1-p)}\] and \[\Pr(S\mid c)=\frac{\varepsilon p}{\varepsilon p+(1-\varepsilon)(1-p)}.\] After signal \(c\), Player 2's expected payoff from \(S\) is \(2\Pr(S\mid c)+3\Pr(C\mid c)\), and from \(C\) is \(1\Pr(S\mid c)+4\Pr(C\mid c)\). Indifference requires \(\Pr(S\mid c)=\Pr(C\mid c)\), which is equivalent to \[\varepsilon p=(1-\varepsilon)(1-p).\] Solving gives \(p=1-\varepsilon\).

title: A Mixed PBE with Signal-Contingent Mixing
learning_objective: Construct a mixed PBE by combining follower and leader indifference conditions.
preliminaries: Use the noisy-leader game with payoffs \((S,S)=(5,2)\), \((S,C)=(3,1)\), \((C,S)=(6,3)\), \((C,C)=(4,4)\), and signals satisfying \(\Pr(s\mid S)=\Pr(c\mid C)=1-\varepsilon\). Assume \(0<\varepsilon<1/4\).
question: Find a PBE where Player 2 plays \(S\) after signal \(s\) and mixes after signal \(c\).
solution: From Player 2's indifference after signal \(c\), Player 1 must choose \(S\) with probability \(p=1-\varepsilon\). This also makes Player 2 willing to play \(S\) after signal \(s\), since \(p\ge \varepsilon\). Let Player 2 choose \(S\) after signal \(c\) with probability \(q\). Player 1's expected payoff from \(S\) is \[(1-\varepsilon)5+\varepsilon(5q+3(1-q))=5-2\varepsilon(1-q).\] Player 1's expected payoff from \(C\) is \[\varepsilon 6+(1-\varepsilon)(6q+4(1-q))=4+2q+2\varepsilon(1-q).\] Indifference requires \[5-2\varepsilon(1-q)=4+2q+2\varepsilon(1-q),\] hence \[q=\frac{1-4\varepsilon}{2-4\varepsilon}.\] This lies in \((0,1)\) for \(0<\varepsilon<1/4\). The PBE is: Player 1 plays \(S\) with probability \(1-\varepsilon\); Player 2 plays \(S\) after \(s\); after \(c\), Player 2 plays \(S\) with probability \((1-4\varepsilon)/(2-4\varepsilon)\). Beliefs are determined by Bayes' rule on path.

title: No PBE with a Mixing Leader and Pure Follower
learning_objective: Rule out a class of PBEs by showing the leader cannot be indifferent.
preliminaries: In the noisy-leader game, Player 1's payoff is \(5\) at \((S,S)\), \(3\) at \((S,C)\), \(6\) at \((C,S)\), and \(4\) at \((C,C)\). A mixed strategy by Player 1 requires indifference between \(S\) and \(C\).
question: Can there be a PBE in which Player 1 mixes and Player 2 uses a pure action after every signal?
solution: No. If Player 2's pure strategy prescribes \(S\) after both signals, then Player 1 gets \(5\) from \(S\) and \(6\) from \(C\), so Player 1 strictly prefers \(C\). If Player 2's pure strategy prescribes \(C\) after both signals, then Player 1 gets \(3\) from \(S\) and \(4\) from \(C\), so Player 1 strictly prefers \(C\). If Player 2's pure strategy depends on the signal, Player 1's expected payoff from \(C\) exceeds that from \(S\) state by state by one whenever the same follower action is induced. In every pure follower strategy, Player 1 cannot be indifferent between \(S\) and \(C\). Therefore Player 1 cannot mix in a PBE with pure follower behavior.

title: Positive Expected Return Need Not Attract a Log-Utility Investor
learning_objective: Show that positive expected monetary return is not sufficient for project acceptance under log utility.
preliminaries: An expected-utility maximizer has initial wealth \(1\) and Bernoulli utility \(u(w)=\log w\). A project pays \(\alpha>0\) with probability \(1/2\) and \(\beta>0\) with probability \(1/2\). Not investing leaves wealth \(1\).
question: Is \((\alpha+\beta)/2>1\) sufficient to guarantee that the individual accepts the all-or-nothing project?
solution: No. Expected utility from not investing is \(\log 1=0\). Expected utility from investing is \[\frac{1}{2}\log\alpha+\frac{1}{2}\log\beta=\log\sqrt{\alpha\beta}.\] The investor accepts only if \(\alpha\beta>1\). Positive expected return requires \(\alpha+\beta>2\), which does not imply \(\alpha\beta>1\). For example, \(\alpha=4\) and \(\beta=1/8\) give \((\alpha+\beta)/2=17/8>1\), but \(\alpha\beta=1/2<1\), so expected utility from investing is negative. Thus positive expected return is not sufficient.

title: Necessary and Sufficient Acceptance Condition under Log Utility
learning_objective: Derive the exact acceptance condition for a binary risky project under log utility.
preliminaries: An investor has utility \(\log w\), initial wealth \(1\), and can either keep wealth \(1\) or invest in a project paying \(\alpha>0\) or \(\beta>0\), each with probability \(1/2\).
question: Give a necessary and sufficient condition on \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) for accepting the project strictly.
solution: The investor is indifferent to not investing at utility \(0\). Investing gives expected utility \[EU=\frac{1}{2}\log\alpha+\frac{1}{2}\log\beta=\log\sqrt{\alpha\beta}.\] Because \(\log\) is strictly increasing, \(EU>0\) if and only if \(\sqrt{\alpha\beta}>1\), equivalently \(\alpha\beta>1\). If \(\alpha\beta<1\), the project is rejected; if \(\alpha\beta=1\), the investor is indifferent. Thus strict acceptance is equivalent to \(\alpha\beta>1\).

title: Positive Fractional Investment under Log Utility
learning_objective: Show that positive expected return implies a positive optimal investment share from zero.
preliminaries: The investor has initial wealth \(1\), utility \(\log w\), and may invest fraction \(\delta\in[0,1]\). Wealth is \(1-\delta+\delta\alpha\) with probability \(1/2\) and \(1-\delta+\delta\beta\) with probability \(1/2\). Assume \((\alpha+\beta)/2>1\) and wealth remains positive for small \(\delta\).
question: Will the investor choose a positive fraction of wealth to invest?
solution: Expected utility is \[V(\delta)=\frac{1}{2}\log(1+\delta(\alpha-1))+\frac{1}{2}\log(1+\delta(\beta-1)).\] At \(\delta=0\), \(V(0)=0\). The right derivative at zero is \[V'(0)=\frac{1}{2}(\alpha-1)+\frac{1}{2}(\beta-1)=\frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}-1>0.\] Therefore there is some sufficiently small \(\delta>0\) such that \(V(\delta)>V(0)\). Hence \(\delta=0\) is not optimal, and any optimum invests a strictly positive fraction.

title: Counterexample to Truthfulness in a Two-Unit Second-Highest-Bid Auction
learning_objective: Disprove dominant-strategy truthfulness in a two-unit auction with second-highest-bid pricing.
preliminaries: There are two identical units and at least three unit-demand bidders. The two highest bidders each win one unit and each winner pays the second-highest bid. A bidder with value \(v_i\) who pays \(p\) receives payoff \(v_i-p\); a loser receives zero.
question: Is truthful bidding weakly dominant? If not, provide a counterexample.
solution: Truthful bidding is not weakly dominant. Take three bidders with values \(v_1=1/3\), \(v_2=1/2\), and \(v_3=1\). Suppose bidders 1 and 3 bid truthfully. If bidder 2 bids truthfully, the winning bids are \(1\) and \(1/2\), and the second-highest bid is \(1/2\). Bidder 2 wins and pays \(1/2\), giving payoff zero. If bidder 2 instead bids \(2/5\), the winning bids are \(1\) and \(2/5\), so bidder 2 still wins but pays \(2/5\). Her payoff is \(1/2-2/5=1/10>0\). Thus truthful bidding is not weakly dominant.

title: Truthfulness in a Two-Unit Third-Price Auction
learning_objective: Prove weak dominance of truthful bidding in a two-unit third-price auction.
preliminaries: There are two identical units and at least three unit-demand bidders. The two highest bidders win and each pays the third-highest bid. Fix bidder \(i\)'s value \(v_i\) and all opponents' bids. Let \(r\) be the second-highest bid among the opponents.
question: Is bidding \(b_i=v_i\) weakly dominant? Prove your answer.
solution: Yes. Bidder \(i\) wins exactly when her bid is high enough to enter the top two bids. The relevant threshold is \(r\), the second-highest opponent bid. If she wins, the third-highest overall bid is \(r\), so her payoff is \(v_i-r\). If she loses, her payoff is zero. If \(v_i>r\), truthful bidding wins and gives positive payoff, while losing would give zero and any other winning bid gives the same payoff. If \(v_i<r\), truthful bidding loses and gives zero, while any winning bid would require paying \(r>v_i\), giving negative payoff. If \(v_i=r\), winning and losing both give zero. Therefore truthful bidding is weakly optimal against every opponents' bid profile and is weakly dominant.

title: Writing the Consumer Problem with Possible Nonattainment
learning_objective: State the utility maximization problem carefully when an optimizer may not exist.
preliminaries: A consumer has wealth \(w>0\), price vector \(p\in\mathbb{R}_+^n\), and utility \(u:\mathbb{R}_+^n\to\mathbb{R}\). The budget set is \(B(p,w)=\{x\in\mathbb{R}_+^n:p\cdot x\le w\}\).
question: Write the consumer's utility-maximization problem while accounting for the possibility that a maximum might not exist.
solution: The robust formulation uses a supremum: \[V(p,w)=\sup\{u(x):x\in\mathbb{R}_+^n,\ p\cdot x\le w\}.\] The demand correspondence is then \[X(p,w)=\arg\max\{u(x):x\in\mathbb{R}_+^n,\ p\cdot x\le w\}\] when the supremum is attained. If the supremum is not attained, \(X(p,w)\) is empty even though \(V(p,w)\) may still be well-defined as an extended real number.

title: Existence of Demand under Positive Prices
learning_objective: Prove existence of a utility maximizer when prices are strictly positive.
preliminaries: A consumer has continuous utility \(u:\mathbb{R}_+^n\to\mathbb{R}\), wealth \(w>0\), and price vector \(p\in\mathbb{R}_{++}^n\). The budget set is \(B(p,w)=\{x\ge 0:p\cdot x\le w\}\).
question: Prove that the consumer's problem has a solution. What can fail if prices are only nonnegative?
solution: If \(p_i>0\) for every \(i\), feasibility implies \(0\le x_i\le w/p_i\). Hence \(B(p,w)\) is bounded. It is closed as the intersection of \(\mathbb{R}_+^n\) and the closed half-space \(\{x:p\cdot x\le w\}\). Therefore \(B(p,w)\) is compact. By the Weierstrass extreme value theorem, continuous \(u\) attains a maximum on \(B(p,w)\). If some price is zero, the budget set can be unbounded. For example, if \(p_1=0\) and \(u(x)=x_1\), utility is unbounded above on the budget set, so no solution exists.

title: Budget Exhaustion under Monotonicity
learning_objective: Prove that a monotone consumer spends all wealth at strictly positive prices.
preliminaries: Prices satisfy \(p\in\mathbb{R}_{++}^n\), wealth is \(w>0\), and \(x^*\) is an optimal bundle. Strong monotonicity means \(x\ge y\) and \(x\ne y\) imply \(u(x)>u(y)\). Strict monotonicity means \(x\gg y\) implies \(u(x)>u(y)\).
question: Show that the budget constraint binds under strong monotonicity. Does the proof change if utility is only strictly increasing?
solution: Suppose \(p\cdot x^*<w\). Let \(\varepsilon=w-p\cdot x^*>0\), and define \[x'=x^*+\frac{\varepsilon}{2\sum_i p_i}(1,\ldots,1).\] Then \(x'\gg x^*\) and \[p\cdot x'=p\cdot x^*+\frac{\varepsilon}{2\sum_i p_i}\sum_i p_i=p\cdot x^*+\varepsilon/2<w.\] Thus \(x'\) is feasible and strictly preferred under strong monotonicity, contradicting optimality. The same construction also works under strict monotonicity, because \(x'\gg x^*\). Hence the budget binds under either condition.

title: Strict Quasiconcavity Implies Unique Demand
learning_objective: Prove single-valued demand from strict quasiconcavity on a convex budget set.
preliminaries: Let \(B(p,w)=\{x\ge 0:p\cdot x\le w\}\), with \(p\in\mathbb{R}_{++}^n\). Utility is continuous and strictly quasiconcave: for distinct \(x,y\) and \(\lambda\in(0,1)\), \(u(\lambda x+(1-\lambda)y)>\min\{u(x),u(y)\}\).
question: Assuming a maximizer exists, prove that the demand correspondence is single-valued. What changes if utility is not strongly increasing?
solution: Suppose two distinct bundles \(x\) and \(y\) both maximize utility on \(B(p,w)\), and let the maximum value be \(V\). Since the budget set is convex, \(z=\lambda x+(1-\lambda)y\) is feasible for \(\lambda\in(0,1)\). Strict quasiconcavity gives \(u(z)>\min\{u(x),u(y)\}=V\), a contradiction. Thus the maximizer is unique. Strong monotonicity is not needed for this uniqueness result. Without strong monotonicity, the unique bundle may fail to exhaust the budget, but strict quasiconcavity still rules out two distinct maximizers.

title: Why Demand Has No General Monotone Price Response
learning_objective: Explain why the standard consumer problem does not directly satisfy Milgrom-Shannon hypotheses.
preliminaries: Milgrom-Shannon monotone comparative statics requires ordered feasible sets with lattice structure and suitable single-crossing or increasing-differences conditions. A Walrasian budget set is \(B(p,w)=\{x\in\mathbb{R}_+^n:p\cdot x\le w\}\).
question: Can we generally say how demand changes with prices under continuous, strongly increasing, strictly quasiconcave utility? Can Milgrom-Shannon's theorem be directly applied?
solution: There is no general coordinatewise monotonicity conclusion for Marshallian demand. A price increase combines substitution and wealth effects; own demand can even rise for a Giffen good, and cross-price effects can have either sign. The listed assumptions give existence, budget exhaustion, and uniqueness, but not monotone demand in prices. Milgrom-Shannon does not directly apply because the budget set is generally not a lattice. In two goods, two feasible bundles can have a componentwise join that violates the budget constraint. Therefore \(B(p,w)\) is not closed under joins and does not satisfy the required feasible-set structure. Demand comparative statics require other tools or stronger assumptions.

title: Profit Function with Input-Augmenting Productivity
learning_objective: Write the profit function when a productivity parameter scales effective input use and output.
preliminaries: A firm has output price \(p>0\), input prices \(w=(w_1,w_2)\gg 0\), and chooses inputs \(x=(x_1,x_2)\in\mathbb{R}_+^2\). Output is \(y=f(\delta x_1,x_2)\), where \(\delta\ge 0\). The technology satisfies \(f(\delta x_1,x_2)=g(\delta)f(x_1,x_2)\), with \(g(0)=0\), \(g(1)=1\), and \(g'(\delta)>0\) for \(\delta>0\).
question: Write the firm's profit function \(\pi(p,w,\delta)\).
solution: Profit is revenue less input cost, maximized over feasible input vectors. Therefore \[\pi(p,w,\delta)=\max_{x_1,x_2\ge 0}\left\{p f(\delta x_1,x_2)-w_1x_1-w_2x_2\right\}.\] Using the assumed scaling property of the technology, this can equivalently be written as \[\pi(p,w,\delta)=\max_{x_1,x_2\ge 0}\left\{p g(\delta)f(x_1,x_2)-w_1x_1-w_2x_2\right\}.\] This expression makes clear that \(\delta\) acts like a multiplicative increase in the effective output price \(p g(\delta)\) for the base technology \(f(x_1,x_2)\).

title: Envelope Derivative of Profit with Respect to Productivity
learning_objective: Use the envelope theorem to sign the derivative of optimized profit with respect to a productivity parameter.
preliminaries: Use the profit function \(\pi(p,w,\delta)=\max_{x\ge 0}\{p g(\delta)f(x)-w\cdot x\}\). Assume the maximizer \(x^*(p,w,\delta)\gg 0\) exists, \(f(x)\ge 0\), \(p>0\), and \(g'(\delta)>0\) for \(\delta>0\).
question: Show that \(\partial \pi(p,w,\delta)/\partial \delta\ge 0\) for \(\delta>0\).
solution: By the envelope theorem, the derivative of the value function with respect to \(\delta\) is the partial derivative of the objective with respect to \(\delta\), evaluated at the optimizer, holding the optimizer fixed. Hence \[\frac{\partial \pi(p,w,\delta)}{\partial \delta}=p g'(\delta) f(x^*(p,w,\delta)).\] Since \(p>0\), \(g'(\delta)>0\), and the production function maps into \(\mathbb{R}_+\), this derivative is nonnegative. It is strictly positive whenever the optimal output of the base technology satisfies \(f(x^*(p,w,\delta))>0\).

title: Output Response to Input-Augmenting Productivity
learning_objective: Relate an increase in a productivity parameter to output supply through the convexity of the profit function in output price.
preliminaries: Let \(r=p g(\delta)\). For the base technology, define \(\varphi(r,w)=\max_{x\ge 0}\{r f(x)-w\cdot x\}\). Assume the maximizer is unique and interior, so the supplied base output \(y(r,w)=f(x^*(r,w))\) is well defined. Since \(g'(\delta)>0\), increasing \(\delta\) increases \(r\).
question: If input one becomes more productive, so \(\delta\) increases, what happens to the profit-maximizing output? Prove your answer.
solution: The profit problem can be rewritten as \(\varphi(r,w)\) with \(r=p g(\delta)\). For fixed \(w\), \(\varphi(r,w)\) is the pointwise supremum of affine functions of \(r\), so it is convex in \(r\). By Hotelling's lemma, where differentiable, \[\frac{\partial \varphi(r,w)}{\partial r}=f(x^*(r,w))=y(r,w).\] The derivative of a convex function is nondecreasing. Therefore \(y(r,w)\) is nondecreasing in \(r\). Because \(r=p g(\delta)\) and \(p g'(\delta)>0\), \(r\) is increasing in \(\delta\). Thus the profit-maximizing output is nondecreasing as \(\delta\) rises. If \(\varphi\) is not differentiable, the same conclusion holds for the output-supply correspondence in the monotone comparative statics sense: its selections are ordered by the increasing subdifferential of the convex profit function.

validation.notes: The conclusion follows from Hotelling's lemma and monotonicity of derivatives of convex value functions.

title: Incompleteness of a Two-Inequality Preference Relation
learning_objective: Disprove completeness by finding two bundles that the preference relation cannot rank.
preliminaries: Bundles are \(x=(x_1,x_2)\in\mathbb{R}_+^2\). Define \(x\succeq y\) if and only if both \(2x_1+x_2\ge 2y_1+y_2\) and \(x_1+2x_2\ge y_1+2y_2\). Completeness means that for every pair \(x,y\), either \(x\succeq y\), \(y\succeq x\), or both.
question: Are these preferences complete? Explain.
solution: They are not complete. Take \(x=(1,0)\) and \(y=(0,1)\). To have \(x\succeq y\), both defining inequalities must hold. The first gives \(2\ge 1\), but the second gives \(1\ge 2\), which is false. Hence \(x\not\succeq y\). To have \(y\succeq x\), the first inequality gives \(1\ge 2\), which is false, even though the second gives \(2\ge 1\). Hence \(y\not\succeq x\). Since this pair is not ranked in either direction, the relation is not complete.

title: Transitivity of a Two-Inequality Preference Relation
learning_objective: Prove transitivity for a preference relation defined as the intersection of two complete preorder comparisons.
preliminaries: Bundles are \(x=(x_1,x_2)\in\mathbb{R}_+^2\). Define \(x\succeq y\) if and only if both \(2x_1+x_2\ge 2y_1+y_2\) and \(x_1+2x_2\ge y_1+2y_2\). Transitivity means that \(x\succeq y\) and \(y\succeq z\) imply \(x\succeq z\).
question: Do these preferences satisfy transitivity? Explain.
solution: Yes. Suppose \(x\succeq y\) and \(y\succeq z\). From \(x\succeq y\), \[2x_1+x_2\ge 2y_1+y_2,\qquad x_1+2x_2\ge y_1+2y_2.\] From \(y\succeq z\), \[2y_1+y_2\ge 2z_1+z_2,\qquad y_1+2y_2\ge z_1+2z_2.\] Chaining the first pair of inequalities gives \(2x_1+x_2\ge 2z_1+z_2\). Chaining the second pair gives \(x_1+2x_2\ge z_1+2z_2\). Both inequalities required for \(x\succeq z\) hold, so the relation is transitive.

title: Expected Utility Maximization with Actuarially Fair Insurance
learning_objective: Formulate the insurance choice problem with an actuarially fair premium.
preliminaries: An individual has initial wealth \(w\), faces loss \(L>0\) with probability \(p\in(0,1)\), and has no loss with probability \(1-p\). Insurance covers fraction \(\alpha\in[0,1]\) of the loss and costs premium \(\alpha pL\). Utility \(u\) is increasing, strictly concave, and twice continuously differentiable.
question: Write the individual's maximization problem.
solution: If the loss occurs, final wealth is initial wealth minus the premium, minus the uncovered part of the loss: \[W_L(\alpha)=w-\alpha pL-(1-\alpha)L.\] If no loss occurs, final wealth is initial wealth minus the premium: \[W_N(\alpha)=w-\alpha pL.\] The expected utility maximization problem is therefore \[\max_{\alpha\in[0,1]}\; p u\left(w-\alpha pL-(1-\alpha)L\right)+(1-p)u\left(w-\alpha pL\right).\]

title: Full Insurance under Actuarially Fair Pricing
learning_objective: Prove that a risk-averse expected utility maximizer fully insures at an actuarially fair premium.
preliminaries: Use the insurance problem \(\max_{\alpha\in[0,1]} p u(W_L(\alpha))+(1-p)u(W_N(\alpha))\), where \(W_L(\alpha)=w-\alpha pL-(1-\alpha)L\) and \(W_N(\alpha)=w-\alpha pL\). Utility is strictly increasing and strictly concave.
question: What is the optimal insurance coverage fraction \(\alpha\)? Prove your answer.
solution: The derivative of expected utility with respect to \(\alpha\) is \[p(1-p)L u'(W_L(\alpha))-(1-p)pL u'(W_N(\alpha))=p(1-p)L\left[u'(W_L(\alpha))-u'(W_N(\alpha))\right].\] For \(\alpha<1\), \(W_L(\alpha)=W_N(\alpha)-(1-\alpha)L<W_N(\alpha)\). Since \(u\) is strictly concave, \(u'\) is strictly decreasing, so \(u'(W_L(\alpha))>u'(W_N(\alpha))\). Thus expected utility is strictly increasing for every \(\alpha<1\). The optimal choice is therefore the boundary \[\alpha^*=1.\] At \(\alpha=1\), final wealth equals \(w-pL\) in both states, eliminating all risk. This is exactly what risk aversion implies under actuarially fair insurance.

validation.notes: Derivative sign shows strict improvement until full insurance.

title: Loss Reduction with Fair Insurance
learning_objective: Show how actuarially fair insurance separates the insurance decision from the efficient loss-reduction decision.
preliminaries: After choosing loss-reduction spending \(x\ge 0\), the loss is \(L(x)\), where \(L(x)>0\), \(L'(x)\le 0\), and \(L''(x)\ge 0\). Insurance covers fraction \(\alpha\in[0,1]\) at premium \(\alpha pL(x)\). Utility is strictly increasing and strictly concave.
question: What can be said about the optimal choices of loss-reduction spending \(x\) and insurance coverage \(\alpha\)? Provide formal analysis and intuition.
solution: For fixed \(x\), the insurance problem is the same fair-insurance problem with loss \(L(x)\). If \(L(x)>0\), strict risk aversion and actuarially fair pricing imply \(\alpha^*(x)=1\). If \(L(x)=0\), insurance is payoff-irrelevant. With full insurance, final wealth is the same in both states and equals \[w-x-pL(x).\] Therefore the choice of \(x\) maximizes \(u(w-x-pL(x))\), which is equivalent, because \(u\) is strictly increasing, to minimizing \[x+pL(x).\] An interior optimum satisfies \[1+pL'(x^*)=0.\] Because \(L''(x)\ge 0\), the function \(x+pL(x)\) is convex, so this first-order condition is sufficient when an interior solution exists. If no interior solution exists, the optimum is at a boundary, for example \(x^*=0\) when \(1+pL'(0)\ge 0\). Intuitively, full insurance removes risk, while loss reduction is chosen only up to the point where one more dollar of prevention equals the expected reduction in loss, \(-pL'(x)\).

validation.notes: Full insurance for fixed x and the prevention FOC follow from strict concavity and monotonicity.

title: Choice from a Union of Menus
learning_objective: Prove a menu-union identity for choices generated by a rational preference relation.
preliminaries: Let \(X\) be a nonempty finite set and let \(\succeq\) be complete and transitive on \(X\). For a nonempty menu \(A\subseteq X\), define \(C(A,\succeq)=\{x\in A:x\succeq a\text{ for all }a\in A\}\).
question: Is the claim \(C(A\cup B,\succeq)=C(C(A,\succeq)\cup C(B,\succeq),\succeq)\) correct for all nonempty \(A,B\subseteq X\)? Prove or give a counterexample.
solution:
The claim is correct. First, take \(x\in C(A\cup B,\succeq)\). Then \(x\succeq y\) for every \(y\in A\cup B\), so in particular \(x\) is at least as good as every element of \(C(A,\succeq)\cup C(B,\succeq)\). Since \(x\in A\cup B\), if \(x\in A\) then \(x\in C(A,\succeq)\), and if \(x\in B\) then \(x\in C(B,\succeq)\). Hence \(x\) belongs to the right-hand choice set.

Conversely, take \(x\in C(C(A,\succeq)\cup C(B,\succeq),\succeq)\). Then \(x\) is a best element of either \(A\) or \(B\), so \(x\in A\cup B\). For any \(y\in A\cup B\), if \(y\in A\), choose some \(a^*\in C(A,\succeq)\). Since \(a^*\succeq y\) and \(x\succeq a^*\), transitivity gives \(x\succeq y\). The same argument applies if \(y\in B\), using an element of \(C(B,\succeq)\). Thus \(x\succeq y\) for all \(y\in A\cup B\), so \(x\in C(A\cup B,\succeq)\).

source.original_question: For rational preferences on finite X, is C(A union B)=C(C(A) union C(B)) correct? Provide a proof or counterexample.
source.notes: Converted from the answer key; reverse inclusion proof expanded to use transitivity correctly.

validation.notes: Both inclusions verified; finite nonempty menus guarantee nonempty choice sets.

title: Strictly Positive Investment in a Favorable Risky Asset
learning_objective: Show that a risky asset with positive expected return receives a strictly positive optimal investment.
preliminaries: An investor with wealth \(w>0\) chooses \(z\in[0,w]\). The asset return is \(H>0\) in the high state and \(L\in(-1,0)\) in the low state, with probabilities \(p\) and \(1-p\). Expected return is positive: \(pH+(1-p)L>0\). Utility \(u\) is strictly increasing, strictly concave, twice continuously differentiable, and satisfies the Inada condition at zero.
question: Show that the individual's optimal investment \(z^*\) in the risky asset is strictly positive.
solution: Final wealth is \(w+zH\) in the high state and \(w+zL\) in the low state. Expected utility is \[F(z)=p u(w+zH)+(1-p)u(w+zL).\] Its right derivative at \(z=0\) is \[F'(0)=pH u'(w)+(1-p)L u'(w)=u'(w)\left[pH+(1-p)L\right]>0.\] Thus moving a small amount from \(z=0\) into the risky asset strictly raises expected utility. Therefore \(z=0\) cannot be optimal, and every optimum satisfies \(z^*>0\). Concavity of \(F\) ensures this local improvement argument is consistent with the global problem.

title: Increasing Differences Imply Single Crossing
learning_objective: Prove that increasing differences is stronger than the single-crossing property.
preliminaries: Let \(X,T\subseteq\mathbb{R}\). A function \(f:X\times T\to\mathbb{R}\) has increasing differences if for \(x'>x\) and \(t'>t\), \(f(x',t')-f(x,t')\ge f(x',t)-f(x,t)\). It has the single-crossing property if \(f(x',t)\ge f(x,t)\) implies \(f(x',t')\ge f(x,t')\), with strict implication preserved for strict inequalities.
question: Prove that increasing differences imply the single-crossing property.
solution: Assume \(f\) has increasing differences. Fix \(x'>x\) and \(t'>t\). If \(f(x',t)\ge f(x,t)\), then \(f(x',t)-f(x,t)\ge 0\). Increasing differences gives \[f(x',t')-f(x,t')\ge f(x',t)-f(x,t)\ge 0.\] Hence \(f(x',t')\ge f(x,t')\). If the premise is strict, then \(f(x',t)-f(x,t)>0\), so the same inequality yields \(f(x',t')-f(x,t')>0\). Thus the weak and strict single-crossing implications both hold.

source.original_question: Prove that the single-crossing property is weaker than increasing differences.
source.notes: Converted from the answer key's direct implication proof.

validation.notes: The proof directly applies the defining inequality for increasing differences.

title: Single Crossing under Increasing Transformations
learning_objective: Show that the single-crossing property is ordinal under strictly increasing transformations.
preliminaries: Let \(f:X\times T\to\mathbb{R}\) satisfy the single-crossing property. Let \(\phi:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\) be strictly increasing and define \(h(x,t)=\phi(f(x,t))\).
question: Prove that if \(f\) has the single-crossing property in \((x,t)\), then any strictly increasing transformation \(h=\phi\circ f\) also has the single-crossing property.
solution: Fix \(x'>x\) and \(t'>t\). Because \(\phi\) is strictly increasing, it preserves order: \(h(x',t)\ge h(x,t)\) if and only if \(f(x',t)\ge f(x,t)\), and strict inequalities are also preserved. Suppose \(h(x',t)\ge h(x,t)\). Then \(f(x',t)\ge f(x,t)\). Since \(f\) is single-crossing, \(f(x',t')\ge f(x,t')\). Applying the increasing transformation again gives \(h(x',t')\ge h(x,t')\). The same argument with strict inequalities proves the strict single-crossing implication. Therefore \(h\) has the single-crossing property.

source.original_question: Prove that if f has the single-crossing property, then any strictly increasing transformation of f has the single-crossing property.
source.notes: Converted from the order-preservation argument in the answer key.

validation.notes: Strict monotonicity preserves weak and strict order comparisons, which is exactly what single crossing uses.

title: First-Order Welfare Effect of Offset Price Changes
learning_objective: Use the indirect utility function to sign the first-order welfare effect of changing two prices in opposite directions.
preliminaries: A consumer has strictly increasing, strictly quasi-concave, twice continuously differentiable utility and interior Marshallian demand \(x^*(p,w)=(x_1^*,x_2^*)\). Initial prices are \(p=(p_1,p_2)\gg 0\), wealth is \(w>0\), and indirect utility is \(V(p,w)\). A policy changes prices to \(\tilde p_1=(1-\alpha)p_1\) and \(\tilde p_2=(1+\alpha)p_2\) for small \(\alpha>0\).
question: Relate the first-order effect of the policy on welfare to the consumer's initial spending on each good.
solution: By the envelope theorem for the utility maximization problem, \(\partial V/\partial p_i=-\lambda x_i^*\), where \(\lambda>0\) is the marginal utility of wealth at the initial optimum. Differentiating \(V((1-\alpha)p_1,(1+\alpha)p_2,w)\) at \(\alpha=0\) gives \[\left.\frac{dV}{d\alpha}\right|_{0}=\frac{\partial V}{\partial p_1}(-p_1)+\frac{\partial V}{\partial p_2}(p_2)=\lambda p_1x_1^*-\lambda p_2x_2^*.\] Therefore the first-order welfare effect is positive if \(p_1x_1^*>p_2x_2^*\), negative if \(p_1x_1^*<p_2x_2^*\), and zero to first order if \(p_1x_1^*=p_2x_2^*\). The policy is beneficial to first order when the consumer initially spends more on the subsidized good than on the good whose price rises.

source.original_question: Relate how the price program affects consumer welfare to the amount spent on each good before the program.
source.notes: Converted from the indirect-utility envelope calculation; corrected the stated second price change to (1+alpha)p2.

validation.notes: Envelope signs and first-order comparison by initial expenditure shares are verified.

title: Compensating Transfer and Revealed Preference
learning_objective: Use affordability of the old bundle to show that a compensated price change weakly raises welfare.
preliminaries: Let \(x^*=(x_1^*,x_2^*)\) solve the original consumer problem at prices \(p=(p_1,p_2)\) and wealth \(w\). New prices are \(\tilde p_1=(1-\alpha)p_1\) and \(\tilde p_2=(1+\alpha)p_2\). The transfer is \(T=\alpha p_2x_2^* - \alpha p_1x_1^*\), so new wealth is \(w+T\).
question: With the new effective prices and transfer, does consumer welfare increase, decrease, or remain unchanged? Explain carefully.
solution: Evaluate the cost of the original bundle under the new prices: \[\tilde p_1x_1^*+\tilde p_2x_2^*=(1-\alpha)p_1x_1^*+(1+\alpha)p_2x_2^*=p_1x_1^*+p_2x_2^*+\alpha p_2x_2^* - \alpha p_1x_1^*.\] Since the original bundle exhausts the original budget under monotonicity, \(p_1x_1^*+p_2x_2^*=w\). Thus \[\tilde p\cdot x^*=w+T.\] The original bundle is exactly affordable after the price change and transfer. Therefore the consumer can attain at least the original utility, so welfare weakly increases. It may strictly increase because the compensated budget line pivots through the original bundle, and another newly affordable bundle may be strictly preferred.

source.original_question: With the price changes and transfer T, does consumer welfare increase, decrease, or remain unchanged?
source.notes: Converted from the answer key's affordability argument.

validation.notes: The old bundle is exactly affordable under the compensated budget, giving weak welfare improvement.

title: Log Utility Demand for State-Contingent Assets
learning_objective: Solve for optimal current consumption and purchases of Arrow-style state assets with log utility.
preliminaries: At date 0 the individual has wealth \(w>0\). Current consumption \(x_0\) has price 1. There are states \(s=1,\ldots,S\) with probabilities \(p(s)>0\) summing to one. Asset \(s\) costs \(q(s)>0\), pays \(r(s)>0\) only in state \(s\), and the individual buys \(x(s)>0\). Utility is \(\ln x_0+\delta\sum_s p(s)\ln(x(s)r(s))\), with \(0<\delta\le 1\).
question: Find optimal current consumption \(x_0\) and optimal asset purchases \(x(s)\).
solution: The budget constraint is \[x_0+\sum_{s=1}^S q(s)x(s)=w.\] The Lagrangian is \[\mathcal{L}=\ln x_0+\delta\sum_s p(s)\ln(x(s)r(s))+\lambda\left(w-x_0-\sum_s q(s)x(s)\right).\] The first-order conditions are \(1/x_0=\lambda\) and \(\delta p(s)/x(s)=\lambda q(s)\) for each state \(s\). Hence \[x(s)=\frac{\delta p(s)x_0}{q(s)}.\] Substituting into the budget gives \[x_0+\sum_s q(s)\frac{\delta p(s)x_0}{q(s)}=x_0+\delta x_0\sum_s p(s)=(1+\delta)x_0=w.\] Thus \[x_0^*=\frac{w}{1+\delta},\qquad x^*(s)=\frac{\delta p(s)w}{(1+\delta)q(s)}.\] The payoff multiplier \(r(s)\) affects utility levels but not asset quantities because it enters the logarithm additively as \(\ln r(s)\).

source.original_question: Find optimal consumption at date 0 and optimal asset purchases at date 0.
source.notes: Converted from the Lagrangian solution in the answer key.

validation.notes: FOCs and budget substitution yield the stated closed-form demands.

title: Indirect Utility and Marginal Utility of Wealth with State Assets
learning_objective: Compute indirect utility and identify marginal utility of wealth after solving a state-contingent asset allocation problem.
preliminaries: Use the optimal choices \(x_0^*=w/(1+\delta)\) and \(x^*(s)=\delta p(s)w/((1+\delta)q(s))\) from the log-utility state-asset problem. State probabilities sum to one and all prices, payoffs, and probabilities are strictly positive.
question: Compute the indirect utility function as a function of wealth, prices, probabilities, and parameters. What is the marginal utility of wealth?
solution: Substituting the optimal choices into utility gives \[V(w,q,r,p,\delta)=\ln\left(\frac{w}{1+\delta}\right)+\delta\sum_s p(s)\ln\left(\frac{\delta p(s)w r(s)}{(1+\delta)q(s)}\right).\] This can be rearranged as \[V=(1+\delta)\ln w-(1+\delta)\ln(1+\delta)+\delta\ln\delta+\delta\sum_s p(s)\left[\ln p(s)+\ln r(s)-\ln q(s)\right].\] The only terms depending on \(w\) are summarized by \((1+\delta)\ln w\), so \[\frac{\partial V}{\partial w}=\frac{1+\delta}{w}.\] This is also the Lagrange multiplier from the budget constraint because \(\lambda=1/x_0^*=(1+\delta)/w\).

source.original_question: Compute the indirect utility function and marginal utility of wealth.
source.notes: Converted from the substitution calculation in the answer key.

validation.notes: Substitution and derivative with respect to wealth are algebraically verified.

title: Threshold for Buying a Uniform Asset-Payoff Upgrade
learning_objective: Use indirect utility to compute the minimum payoff multiplier that compensates for a reduction in initial wealth.
preliminaries: In the state-asset problem, indirect utility can be written as \(V=(1+\delta)\ln w+K(r,q,p,\delta)\), where \(K\) collects terms independent of wealth except for the payoff vector. The individual can pay 1 unit of wealth, reducing wealth from \(w>1\) to \(w-1\), to multiply every payoff \(r(s)\) by \(\alpha>1\).
question: What is the smallest value of \(\alpha\) for which the individual accepts the opportunity?
solution: Multiplying every payoff \(r(s)\) by \(\alpha\) adds \(\delta\sum_s p(s)\ln\alpha=\delta\ln\alpha\) to indirect utility. The individual accepts if \[(1+\delta)\ln(w-1)+K+\delta\ln\alpha\ge (1+\delta)\ln w+K.\] Cancelling \(K\) and rearranging, \[\delta\ln\alpha\ge (1+\delta)\ln\left(\frac{w}{w-1}\right).\] Therefore the smallest acceptable multiplier is \[\alpha^*=\left(\frac{w}{w-1}\right)^{(1+\delta)/\delta}.\] At this value the individual is exactly indifferent; any larger \(\alpha\) is strictly preferred.

source.original_question: What is the smallest alpha for which the individual accepts spending 1 unit of wealth to multiply all asset payoffs by alpha?
source.notes: Converted from the answer key's indirect-utility comparison.

validation.notes: The threshold follows from cancelling common indirect-utility terms and exponentiating.

title: Existence of a Best Alternative on a Finite Set
learning_objective: Prove that complete and transitive preferences have a maximal element on any finite nonempty set.
preliminaries: Let \(A=\{a_1,\ldots,a_N\}\) be finite and nonempty. A rational preference relation \(\succeq\) is complete and transitive. An alternative \(a^*\in A\) is best in \(A\) if \(a^*\succeq a_i\) for every \(a_i\in A\).
question: Show that a best alternative exists for this decision maker and finite set \(A\).
solution: Proceed by induction on the number of alternatives. For one alternative, that alternative is best. Suppose every set with \(N-1\) alternatives has a best element. Let \(A=\{a_1,\ldots,a_N\}\) and let \(B=\{a_1,\ldots,a_{N-1}\}\). By the induction hypothesis, there is \(b^*\in B\) such that \(b^*\succeq b\) for every \(b\in B\). By completeness, either \(b^*\succeq a_N\) or \(a_N\succeq b^*\), or both. In the first case, \(b^*\) is best in \(A\). In the second case, transitivity gives \(a_N\succeq b^*\succeq b\) for every \(b\in B\), so \(a_N\) is best in \(A\). Thus a best alternative exists for every finite nonempty set.

source.original_question: Show that a best alternative exists for a rational decision maker on a finite set A.
source.notes: Converted from the induction proof in the answer key.

validation.notes: Completeness compares the current best with the new element; transitivity preserves dominance over the old set.

title: Deleting an Alternative Cannot Hurt a Single Decision Maker
learning_objective: Show that removing an alternative from a feasible set cannot make a rational single-agent chooser worse off relative to the original best alternative.
preliminaries: Let \(A=\{a_1,…,a_N\}\) and \(A'=\{a_1,…,a_{N-1}\}\). Let \(a^*\) be a best alternative in \(A\), and let \(a'\) be a best alternative in \(A'\), under the same complete and transitive preference relation \(\succeq\).
question: Show that \(a^*\succeq a'\), and interpret the result.
solution: Because \(A'\subseteq A\), the alternative \(a'\in A'\) is also an element of \(A\). Since \(a^*\) is best in \(A\), it is weakly preferred to every element of \(A\). In particular, \[a^*\succeq a'.\] Thus the best attainable alternative from the original larger set is at least as good as the best attainable alternative after deleting \(a_N\). For an individual decision maker with fixed rational preferences, reducing the feasible set cannot improve the best element relative to what was already available in the larger set; the original best could always have been chosen before the deletion.

source.original_question: Let a' be best in A' after deleting a_N. Show that a* is weakly preferred to a'.
source.notes: Converted from the answer key's set-inclusion argument.

validation.notes: The proof is immediate from the definition of best alternative and set inclusion.

title: Removing a Strategy Can Improve Equilibrium Payoffs
learning_objective: Contrast single-agent menu deletion with strategic effects of removing an action in a game.
preliminaries: Consider the two-player normal-form game. Player 1 chooses row \(a_{11}\) or \(a_{12}\); player 2 chooses column \(a_{21}\) or \(a_{22}\). Payoffs are \((5,5)\) at \((a_{11},a_{21})\), \((0,4)\) at \((a_{11},a_{22})\), \((7,0)\) at \((a_{12},a_{21})\), and \((1,1)\) at \((a_{12},a_{22})\).
question: Find all Nash equilibria of the original game. Then eliminate \(a_{12}\) for player 1 and find all Nash equilibria of the reduced game. Compare this with the single-agent result that deleting an alternative cannot make the decision maker worse off.
solution:
In the original game, player 1's best response to \(a_{21}\) is \(a_{12}\), because \(7>5\). Player 1's best response to \(a_{22}\) is also \(a_{12}\), because \(1>0\). Thus \(a_{12}\) strictly dominates \(a_{11}\) for player 1. Player 2's best response to \(a_{11}\) is \(a_{21}\), because \(5>4\). Player 2's best response to \(a_{12}\) is \(a_{22}\), because \(1>0\). The only mutual best response is \((a_{12},a_{22})\), with payoffs \((1,1)\).

If \(a_{12}\) is removed, player 1 must play \(a_{11}\). Player 2's best response to \(a_{11}\) is \(a_{21}\), so the unique equilibrium of the reduced game is \((a_{11},a_{21})\), with payoffs \((5,5)\). Player 1 is better off in the reduced game equilibrium than in the original game equilibrium. This does not contradict the single-agent deletion result: in a game, removing one player's action can change the other player's equilibrium behavior. The improved outcome comes from strategic interaction, not from the removed action being directly preferred by player 1.

source.original_question: Find all Nash equilibria before and after eliminating a12 for player 1, and compare with the single-agent deletion result.
source.notes: Converted from the answer key's equilibrium comparison.

validation.notes: Best-response checks identify the unique equilibrium in each game and the strategic reason payoffs improve.

title: Observing Full Insurance and the Fair Premium
learning_objective: Infer the insurance price from an observed full-insurance choice by a strictly risk-averse expected utility maximizer.
preliminaries: An individual with wealth \(w>L>0\) loses \(L\) with probability \(\pi\in(0,1)\). One unit of insurance costs \(p\) and pays 1 only in the loss state. The individual can choose any real amount \(x\) of insurance and has strictly increasing, strictly concave expected utility.
question: If the individual chooses \(x=L\) units of insurance, what can be inferred about \(p\) versus \(\pi\)? Prove and give intuition.
solution: Expected utility is \[V(x)=\pi u(w-L+x-px)+(1-\pi)u(w-px).\] At \(x=L\), wealth in both states is \(w-pL\). The derivative is \[V'(x)=\pi(1-p)u'(w-L+(1-p)x)-(1-\pi)p u'(w-px).\] Evaluating at \(x=L\) gives \[V'(L)=u'(w-pL)\left[\pi(1-p)-(1-\pi)p\right]=u'(w-pL)(\pi-p).\] Since utility is strictly concave, the objective is strictly concave, and an interior optimum at \(x=L\) requires \(V'(L)=0\). Therefore \(p=\pi\). Intuitively, full insurance equalizes wealth across states. A strictly risk-averse person chooses exactly full insurance only when insurance is actuarially fair. If \(p<\pi\), insurance is cheap and the person would overinsure; if \(p>\pi\), insurance is expensive and the person would underinsure.

source.original_question: If the individual chooses L units of insurance, what can you infer about p versus pi?
source.notes: Converted from the answer key; equality conclusion stated using the interior unrestricted-choice FOC.

validation.notes: The derivative at full insurance is proportional to pi minus p, so unrestricted interior choice implies equality.

title: Insurance Demand with Log Utility
learning_objective: Derive closed-form insurance demand for a log-utility expected utility maximizer.
preliminaries: An individual has wealth \(w>L>0\), loss probability \(\pi\), and can buy any real amount \(x\) of insurance. One unit costs \(p\in(0,1)\) and pays 1 in the loss state. Bernoulli utility is \(u(W)=\ln W\). Assume the optimum keeps state-contingent wealth positive.
question: How much insurance will this individual purchase? Derive the optimal \(x\); do not assume it equals \(L\).
solution: Expected utility is \[V(x)=\pi\ln(w-L+(1-p)x)+(1-\pi)\ln(w-px).\] The first-order condition is \[\frac{\pi(1-p)}{w-L+(1-p)x}-\frac{(1-\pi)p}{w-px}=0.\] Cross-multiplying, \[\pi(1-p)(w-px)=(1-\pi)p(w-L+(1-p)x).\] Solving for \(x\) gives \[x^*=\frac{p(1-\pi)L+w(\pi-p)}{p(1-p)}.\] The second derivative is negative wherever both state-contingent wealth levels are positive, so this first-order condition is sufficient for an interior optimum. When \(p=\pi\), the expression reduces to \(x^*=L\), the full-insurance result.

source.original_question: Suppose utility is ln. How much insurance will this individual purchase?
source.notes: Converted from the answer key's log-utility FOC; algebra independently corrected to include w, L, p, and pi.

validation.notes: FOC algebra verified; the formula nests full insurance at the fair price.

title: Why Non-Affine Transformations Change Expected Utility Choices
learning_objective: Explain why expected utility representations are unique only up to positive affine transformations, not arbitrary increasing transformations.
preliminaries: A Bernoulli utility function represents preferences over lotteries through expected utility. Two Bernoulli utility functions represent the same von Neumann-Morgenstern preferences over lotteries if and only if they are related by a positive affine transformation \(\tilde u=a u+b\), where \(a>0\).
question: A colleague says the log-utility insurance demand cannot be correct because any strictly increasing transformation represents the same preferences, and a square-root utility gives a different demand. Do you agree? Explain.
solution: Disagree. For preferences over certain wealth levels, any strictly increasing transformation preserves the ordinal ranking of sure amounts. But expected utility preferences are preferences over lotteries, and the Bernoulli utility representation is cardinal up to positive affine transformations only. A nonlinear increasing transformation, such as transforming \(\ln W\) into \(\sqrt{W}\), generally changes attitudes toward risk and therefore changes lottery rankings. Thus a square-root utility function need not represent the same expected utility preferences as log utility. It is not a contradiction that it gives a different insurance demand. The log-utility demand is correct for the expected utility preference whose Bernoulli utility is \(\ln W\); a different non-affine Bernoulli utility describes a different risk preference.

source.original_question: Do you agree that a different answer under square-root utility means the log answer cannot be correct?
source.notes: Converted from the answer key's point about affine transformations for vNM utility.

validation.notes: The distinction between ordinal utility over sure outcomes and vNM utility over lotteries is correctly stated.

title: Bayesian Game Form of a First-Price Auction
learning_objective: Represent a symmetric private-value first-price auction as a static Bayesian game.
preliminaries: Two bidders compete for one indivisible good. Each bidder \(i\) has valuation \(\theta_i\), independently and uniformly distributed on \([0,x]\), with \(x>0\). Bids are simultaneous, nonnegative, and the highest bid wins and pays its bid. Ties are broken with probability one half for each bidder.
question: Represent the auction as a static game of incomplete information: specify players, actions, types, nature's strategy, and payoffs.
solution: The players are \(N=\{1,2\}\). Each action set is \(A_i=[0,\infty)\), where an action is a bid \(b_i\). Each type set is \(T_i=[0,x]\), and type \(\theta_i\in T_i\) is bidder \(i\)'s valuation. Nature independently draws \(\theta_1\) and \(\theta_2\) from the uniform distribution on \([0,x]\), and each bidder observes only her own type. Payoffs are \[u_i(b_i,b_j,\theta_i)=\begin{cases}\theta_i-b_i,& b_i>b_j,\\\frac{\theta_i-b_i}{2},& b_i=b_j,\\0,& b_i<b_j.\end{cases}\] A strategy for bidder \(i\) is a measurable function \(s_i:[0,x]\to[0,\infty)\). The appropriate solution concept is Bayesian Nash equilibrium.

source.original_question: Represent the auction as a static game of incomplete information.
source.notes: Converted from the answer key's Bayesian-game tuple.

validation.notes: Players, types, actions, prior, information, and payoff function are fully specified.

title: Truthful Bidding Is Not a First-Price Auction Equilibrium
learning_objective: Show why bidding one's valuation cannot be a Bayesian Nash equilibrium in a first-price auction.
preliminaries: In the first-price auction, bidder \(i\)'s payoff is \(\theta_i-b_i\) if she wins with bid \(b_i\), half that amount in a tie, and zero if she loses. Types are independently uniform on \([0,x]\).
question: Is it an equilibrium for both bidders to bid their own valuations? Explain.
solution: No. Suppose bidder \(j\) bids truthfully, \(b_j=\theta_j\). If bidder \(i\) of type \(\theta_i>0\) also bids truthfully, she wins when \(\theta_j<\theta_i\) but earns zero surplus whenever she wins because she pays \(b_i=\theta_i\). Her expected payoff is therefore zero. Instead, choose a small \(\varepsilon\in(0,\theta_i)\) and bid \(b_i=\theta_i-\varepsilon\). She wins with probability \((\theta_i-\varepsilon)/x>0\), and conditional on winning earns surplus \(\varepsilon>0\). Her expected payoff is \(\varepsilon(\theta_i-\varepsilon)/x>0\). This profitable deviation shows truthful bidding is not a Bayesian Nash equilibrium in a first-price auction.

source.original_question: Is it an equilibrium for players to bid their own valuations? Explain.
source.notes: Converted from the answer key's underbidding deviation.

validation.notes: A positive-surplus underbid is a profitable deviation for all positive types.

title: Symmetric Linear Equilibrium in a First-Price Auction
learning_objective: Derive the symmetric linear bidding equilibrium in a two-bidder first-price auction with uniform private values.
preliminaries: Two risk-neutral bidders have independent valuations uniformly distributed on \([0,x]\). Bids are nonnegative, the high bid wins, and the winner pays her bid. Consider symmetric increasing strategies of the form \(s(\theta)=\beta\theta\), with \(\beta>0\).
question: Find an equilibrium of the game using strategies of the form \(s_i(\theta_i)=\beta_i\theta_i\).
solution: Suppose bidder 2 uses the increasing linear strategy \(s(\theta_2)=\beta\theta_2\). If bidder 1 with value \(v\) bids \(b\in[0,\beta x]\), she wins when \(\beta\theta_2<b\), which has probability \(b/(\beta x)\). Her expected payoff is \[\frac{b}{\beta x}(v-b).\] Maximizing with respect to \(b\) gives \[\frac{\partial}{\partial b}\left[\frac{b}{\beta x}(v-b)\right]=\frac{v-2b}{\beta x}=0,\] so the best response bid is \(b=v/2\). In a symmetric equilibrium this best response must equal \(\beta v\), so \(\beta=1/2\). Therefore \[s_i(\theta_i)=\frac{\theta_i}{2}\qquad i=1,2\] is the symmetric linear Bayesian Nash equilibrium. The candidate bid lies in \([0,x/2]\), so it is within the range assumed above.

source.original_question: Find an equilibrium using strategies of the form s_i(theta_i)=beta_i theta_i.
source.notes: Converted from the answer key's symmetric linear bidding derivation.

validation.notes: The best-response calculation yields beta equals one half for the symmetric uniform two-bidder first-price auction.

title: Recursive Social Utility on a Three-Person Cycle
learning_objective: Solve a cyclic recursive utility system and express each utility as a weighted sum of log consumptions.
preliminaries: There are three individuals and one consumption good, apples. Consumptions satisfy \(x_i>0\). Let \(s(1)=2\), \(s(2)=3\), and \(s(3)=1\). Assume \(0\le \beta<1\), so the recursive utility system has a finite solution.
question: Each individual has utility \[U_i(x_1,x_2,x_3)=\log x_i+\beta U_{s(i)}(x_1,x_2,x_3).\] Find the functional form of \(U_1\), \(U_2\), and \(U_3\).
solution: The system is \[U_1=\log x_1+\beta U_2,\qquad U_2=\log x_2+\beta U_3,\qquad U_3=\log x_3+\beta U_1.\] Substituting forward, \[U_1=\log x_1+\beta\log x_2+\beta^2\log x_3+\beta^3U_1.\] Since \(1-\beta^3>0\), \[U_1=\frac{\log x_1+\beta\log x_2+\beta^2\log x_3}{1-\beta^3}.\] Cycling indices gives \[U_2=\frac{\log x_2+\beta\log x_3+\beta^2\log x_1}{1-\beta^3},\qquad U_3=\frac{\log x_3+\beta\log x_1+\beta^2\log x_2}{1-\beta^3}.\] Direct substitution of these three formulas into the original recursion verifies the solution.

source.original_question: Consider an economy with 3 individuals. Each individual cares only about apples and about his mates. Utilities satisfy U_i=log x_i + beta U_{s(i)}, with s(1)=2, s(2)=3, and s(3)=1. Individuals 1 and 2 have labor endowment e>1, individual 3 has labor endowment 1, labor is supplied inelastically, and apples are produced from labor one-for-one.
source.notes: Converted from the source answer and independently checked by solving the three-equation recursion.

validation.notes: The cyclic substitution is correct and requires only the explicit condition \(\beta^3\ne 1\); \(0\le \beta<1\) was added for a standard well-defined social-weight interpretation.

title: Competitive Equilibrium with Recursive Consumption Externalities
learning_objective: Compute a competitive equilibrium when agents care about others' consumption but choose only their own consumption.
preliminaries: Use the utility formulas from the three-person cycle with \(0\le \beta<1\). Agents 1 and 2 each supply \(e>1\) units of labor inelastically, and agent 3 supplies one unit. Apples are produced from labor by \(y=l\). Normalize the apple price to \(p=1\), and let \(w\) be the wage.
question: Find a competitive equilibrium for the apple economy with recursive social utility.
solution: Agent \(i\) takes other agents' consumptions and prices as given. In \(U_i\), the only term affected by agent \(i\)'s own apple choice is \(\log x_i/(1-\beta^3)\), so each agent spends all labor income on apples. Thus \[x_1=we,\qquad x_2=we,\qquad x_3=w.\] Total labor supply is \(2e+1\), and one unit of labor produces one apple, so aggregate apple supply is \(2e+1\). Apple-market clearing requires \[we+we+w=w(2e+1)=2e+1.\] Since \(2e+1>0\), \(w=1\). Therefore an equilibrium, with apples as numeraire, is \[p=1,\qquad w=1,\qquad (x_1,x_2,x_3)=(e,e,1),\qquad y=2e+1.\] Labor and apple markets clear, and each consumer maximizes because utility is strictly increasing in own consumption and labor is inelastically supplied.

source.original_question: Consider the three-person apple economy with recursive utilities U_i=log x_i + beta U_{s(i)}, labor endowments e,e,1, inelastic labor supply, and production f(l)=l.
source.notes: Converted from the source solution and independently checked using individual budget exhaustion plus market clearing.

validation.notes: Budget demands, wage normalization, and goods-market clearing all verify the stated allocation.

title: Marginal Incentives Under Consumption Externalities
learning_objective: Explain why payoff externalities need not affect a price-taking consumer's private marginal choice.
preliminaries: Use the three-person utility functions \[U_i=(\log x_i+\beta\log x_{s(i)}+\beta^2\log x_{s(s(i))})/(1-\beta^3)\] with \(0\le \beta<1\). Each consumer chooses only his own apple consumption subject to his own budget and takes others' consumptions as fixed.
question: Does the consumption externality distort any marginal incentives in the competitive equilibrium?
solution: No private marginal incentive is distorted in the consumer's own choice problem. For agent \(i\), the derivative of utility with respect to the consumption he controls is \[\frac{\partial U_i}{\partial x_i}=\frac{1}{(1-\beta^3)x_i}.\] The terms involving other agents' consumptions are constants from agent \(i\)'s perspective, because he cannot choose \(x_{s(i)}\) or \(x_{s(s(i))}\). Therefore the consumer's optimality condition is the same as for a monotone transformation of \(\log x_i\): spend all available income on apples. The externality changes utility levels and social marginal effects, but it does not change the private marginal tradeoff faced by any price-taking individual in this one-good economy.

source.original_question: In the three-person apple economy with recursive utilities, ask whether the consumption externality distorts marginal incentives.
source.notes: Converted from the source answer and narrowed to the private marginal-incentive claim, which can be verified directly from the derivative.

validation.notes: The derivative with respect to own consumption is independent of others' choices except through constants in utility levels.

title: Quasiconvexity of Indirect Utility in Prices and Wealth
learning_objective: Prove quasiconvexity of indirect utility in the price-wealth vector by budget-set inclusion.
preliminaries: A consumer has continuous, convex, monotone preferences on \(\mathbb{R}_+^2\), represented by a utility function \(u\). For \(p\in\mathbb{R}_{++}^2\) and \(w>0\), define \[V(p,w)=\max\{u(x):x\in\mathbb{R}_+^2,\ p\cdot x\le w\}.\]
question: Show that \(V(p,w)\) is quasiconvex in \((p,w)\).
solution: Take two price-wealth pairs \((p,w)\) and \((p',w')\), and let \(\lambda\in[0,1]\). Write \((\bar p,\bar w)=(\lambda p+(1-\lambda)p',\lambda w+(1-\lambda)w')\). If \(x\) is affordable at \((\bar p,\bar w)\), then \[\lambda(p\cdot x-w)+(1-\lambda)(p'\cdot x-w')\le 0.\] It cannot be true that both \(p\cdot x>w\) and \(p'\cdot x>w'\), because then the left side would be positive. Hence \(x\) is affordable at \((p,w)\) or at \((p',w')\). Therefore \[B(\bar p,\bar w)\subseteq B(p,w)\cup B(p',w'),\] where \(B\) denotes the budget set. Maximizing utility over a subset of the union gives \[V(\bar p,\bar w)\le \max\{V(p,w),V(p',w')\}.\] This is exactly quasiconvexity of \(V\) in \((p,w)\).

source.original_question: A consumer has continuous, convex, monotonic preferences on R^2_+, wealth w>0, and prices p in R^2_{++}. Show that indirect utility V(p,w) is quasiconvex in (p,w).
source.notes: Converted from the source answer and independently checked through the budget-set inclusion proof.

validation.notes: The proof establishes the defining quasiconvex inequality directly.

title: Concavity of Indirect Utility in Wealth
learning_objective: Show that a concave direct utility representation implies concavity of indirect utility in wealth.
preliminaries: Fix \(p\in\mathbb{R}_{++}^2\). Preferences are represented by a concave utility function \(u\), and \[V(p,w)=\max\{u(x):x\ge 0,\ p\cdot x\le w\}.\]
question: Show that \(V(p,w)\) is concave in \(w\) for fixed \(p\).
solution: Let \(w,w'>0\), \(\lambda\in[0,1]\), and \(\bar w=\lambda w+(1-\lambda)w'\). Let \(x\) solve the problem at wealth \(w\), and let \(x'\) solve it at wealth \(w'\). The convex combination \(\bar x=\lambda x+(1-\lambda)x'\) is affordable at wealth \(\bar w\), since \[p\cdot \bar x=\lambda p\cdot x+(1-\lambda)p\cdot x'\le \lambda w+(1-\lambda)w'=\bar w.\] Therefore \[V(p,\bar w)\ge u(\bar x).\] By concavity of \(u\), \[u(\bar x)\ge \lambda u(x)+(1-\lambda)u(x')=\lambda V(p,w)+(1-\lambda)V(p,w').\] Combining the inequalities yields \[V(p,\lambda w+(1-\lambda)w')\ge \lambda V(p,w)+(1-\lambda)V(p,w'),\] so \(V\) is concave in wealth.

source.original_question: Suppose the consumer's preferences are represented by a concave utility function. Show that indirect utility V(p,w) is concave in w for fixed p.
source.notes: Converted from the source answer and independently verified via affordable convex combinations.

validation.notes: The affordability and utility-concavity inequalities prove the result without differentiability.

title: Concavity and Quasiconvexity Are Compatible
learning_objective: Explain why indirect utility can be both concave and quasiconvex in wealth.
preliminaries: For fixed prices \(p\), suppose \(V(p,w)\) is concave in \(w\). From quasiconvexity in \((p,w)\), it also satisfies \[V(p,\lambda w+(1-\lambda)w')\le \max\{V(p,w),V(p,w')\}.\]
question: Is it a contradiction that \(V(p,w)\) is quasiconvex in \(w\) for fixed \(p\), while for a concave utility representation it is also concave in \(w\)?
solution: There is no contradiction. Concavity gives a lower bound, \[V(p,\lambda w+(1-\lambda)w')\ge \lambda V(p,w)+(1-\lambda)V(p,w'),\] while quasiconvexity gives an upper bound, \[V(p,\lambda w+(1-\lambda)w')\le \max\{V(p,w),V(p,w')\}.\] These inequalities are compatible because any weighted average of two numbers is weakly below their maximum: \[\lambda V(p,w)+(1-\lambda)V(p,w')\le \max\{V(p,w),V(p,w')\}.\] For example, an increasing concave function of one variable can also be quasiconvex because its value at an intermediate wealth lies between the endpoint values. Thus the two properties restrict \(V\) in different directions but do not conflict.

source.original_question: Since V(p,w) is quasiconvex in (p,w), it is quasiconvex in w for fixed p. If utility is concave, V(p,w) is concave in w for fixed p. Is this a contradiction?
source.notes: Converted from the source answer and independently checked against the definitions of concavity and quasiconvexity.

validation.notes: The weighted-average inequality shows the two bounds can hold simultaneously.

title: Second-Price Auction as a Bayesian Game
learning_objective: State the players, types, actions, beliefs, allocation rule, and payoffs of a private-value auction.
preliminaries: There are \(N\) bidders. Bidder \(i\)'s value \(v_i\) is privately observed, and the values are i.i.d. with common distribution \(F\) on \([\underline V,\overline V]\). Bids must lie in \([\underline V,\overline V]\). The object goes to a highest bidder and the winner pays the second-highest bid.
question: Set up the sealed-bid second-price auction as a Bayesian game played by the bidders.
solution: The Bayesian game is as follows. The player set is \(\{1,\ldots,N\}\). Player \(i\)'s type is \(v_i\in[\underline V,\overline V]\). The common prior is the product distribution generated by independent draws from \(F\), so conditional on \(v_i\), bidder \(i\) believes the other values are independent with cdf \(F\). A pure strategy for bidder \(i\) is a measurable bidding function \(b_i:[\underline V,\overline V]\to[\underline V,\overline V]\). Given realized bids, a highest bidder wins, with ties resolved by the auctioneer's specified tie-breaking rule, and the winner pays the second-highest bid. If bidder \(i\) wins at price \(m_i\), his payoff is \(v_i-m_i\); if he loses, his payoff is \(0\). This specifies types, beliefs, actions, outcomes, and payoffs, so it is the requested Bayesian game.

source.original_question: A seller sells one unit by sealed-bid second-price auction to N bidders with iid private values from F on [V_lower,V_upper]. Each bidder observes only his own value. Set up the auction as a Bayesian game.
