validation.notes: All components of the Bayesian game are explicitly specified.

title: Truthful Bidding in a Second-Price Auction
learning_objective: Prove that truthful bidding is a Bayes-Nash equilibrium in a private-value second-price auction.
preliminaries: Use the sealed-bid second-price auction with private values \(v_i\in[\underline V,\overline V]\). A bidder's payoff is value minus price if he wins and zero if he loses.
question: Find a Bayes-Nash equilibrium of this second-price auction.
solution: The truthful strategy profile \(b_i(v_i)=v_i\) for every bidder \(i\) is a Bayes-Nash equilibrium. In fact, truthful bidding is weakly dominant. Fix bidder \(i\)'s value \(v_i\) and fix the opponents' bids. Let \(m\) be the highest opposing bid. If \(v_i\ge m\), bidding \(v_i\) wins and gives payoff \(v_i-m\ge 0\). Any higher bid also wins at the same price, and any lower bid either still wins at the same price or loses and gives \(0\), so it cannot improve payoff. If \(v_i<m\), bidding \(v_i\) loses and gives payoff \(0\). A lower bid also loses, while a higher bid that wins would require paying \(m>v_i\), giving negative payoff. Thus no bid yields a strictly higher payoff than bidding \(v_i\), for every value and every profile of opponents' bids. Therefore truthful bidding is a best response for every type when others bid truthfully, so it is a Bayes-Nash equilibrium.

source.original_question: Find a Bayes Nash equilibrium of the sealed-bid second-price auction with iid private values.
source.notes: Converted from the source answer and independently checked by the standard dominant-strategy argument.

validation.notes: The case split on the highest opposing bid proves weak dominance and hence BNE.

title: Revenue-Maximizing Shill Bid in a Second-Price Auction
learning_objective: Analyze how an auctioneer can transform a second-price auction by submitting a shill bid after observing bids.
preliminaries: After observing real bids \((b_1,\ldots,b_N)\), the auctioneer may submit a bid to maximize revenue. The auctioneer has no value for keeping the object. If the auctioneer can tie the highest bid and choose a real highest bidder as winner, the supremum revenue is attained; otherwise use an arbitrarily small \(\varepsilon>0\).
question: Given real bids \((b_1,\ldots,b_N)\), what bid should the cheating auctioneer submit, who wins, and what does the winner pay?
solution: Let \(b_{(1)}=\max_i b_i\) be the highest real bid. The auctioneer wants the highest real bidder to win while making the second-highest bid as large as possible. If the auctioneer can bid \(b_{(1)}\) and then use the tie-breaking rule to select a real bidder as winner, he submits \(b_0=b_{(1)}\). The real highest bidder wins and pays \(b_{(1)}\), his own bid. If the rules require the auctioneer not to tie as a winning bidder, the auctioneer instead submits \(b_0=b_{(1)}-\varepsilon\), and the highest real bidder pays \(b_{(1)}-\varepsilon\). Thus the cheating auctioneer makes the winner pay essentially the highest real bid rather than the second-highest real bid.

source.original_question: Suppose the auctioneer announces a second-price auction but cheats by submitting his own bid after opening all real bids. Given real bids, what bid should the auctioneer submit, who wins, and what does the winner pay?
source.notes: Converted from the source answer and independently checked, adding the tie-breaking qualification.

validation.notes: The revenue-maximizing shill bid is at the highest real bid, or arbitrarily close below it if exact tying is unavailable.

title: Truthful Bidding Fails with a Cheating Auctioneer
learning_objective: Show that post-bid shill bidding destroys truthful bidding as an equilibrium.
preliminaries: In the cheating auctioneer version, after real bids are opened the auctioneer submits a shill bid that makes the highest real bidder pay essentially his own bid. Bidders know this rule.
question: Is the truthful Bayes-Nash equilibrium from the honest second-price auction still an equilibrium when the auctioneer cheats? Explain.
solution: No. With the shill bid, the winner pays essentially his own bid, so the strategic incentives are those of a first-price auction. Under truthful bidding, a bidder with value \(v_i>\underline V\) who wins with positive probability pays approximately \(v_i\), earning approximately zero surplus when he wins. A small underbid, such as \(v_i-\varepsilon\), still wins in states where the highest opposing bid is below \(v_i-\varepsilon\), but yields strictly positive surplus \(\varepsilon\) in those winning states. For continuous value distributions, this event has positive probability for interior types. Therefore truthful bidding is not a best response and cannot be a Bayes-Nash equilibrium in the cheating-auctioneer game.

source.original_question: In the cheating-auctioneer version of the announced second-price auction, is truthful bidding still a Bayes Nash equilibrium? Explain.
source.notes: Converted from the source answer and independently checked by constructing a profitable underbidding deviation.

validation.notes: The deviation argument correctly identifies the first-price-like incentives created by the shill bid.

title: Best Replies in an n-Firm Cournot Market
learning_objective: Derive each firm's best response under linear inverse demand with a shutdown boundary.
preliminaries: There are \(n\) firms. Firm \(i\) chooses \(q_i\ge 0\). Let \(Q_{-i}=\sum_{j\ne i}q_j\). Price is \(p=\max\{1-q_i-Q_{-i},0\}\), and marginal cost is \(c\in(0,1)\). Firm \(i\)'s profit is \((p-c)q_i\).
question: Characterize firm \(i\)'s best reply function.
solution: If \(Q_{-i}\ge 1-c\), then for every \(q_i>0\) the margin is weakly negative: when price is positive, \(1-q_i-Q_{-i}-c<0\), and when price is zero, the margin is \(-c<0\). Thus the best response is \(q_i=0\). If \(Q_{-i}<1-c\), the relevant positive-price profit for quantities below the choke point is \[\pi_i(q_i,Q_{-i})=(1-q_i-Q_{-i}-c)q_i.\] This is strictly concave in \(q_i\), and the first-order condition is \[1-Q_{-i}-c-2q_i=0.\] Hence \[BR_i(Q_{-i})=\max\left\{0,\frac{1-c-Q_{-i}}{2}\right\}.\]

source.original_question: In an n-firm Cournot market, firms choose quantities, price is max{1-sum q_i,0}, marginal cost is c in (0,1), and profit is (p-c)q_i. Characterize best replies.
source.notes: Converted from the source solution and independently checked at the boundary where rivals' output makes positive production unprofitable.

validation.notes: The best reply combines the concave interior solution with the correct zero-output boundary.

title: Symmetric Cournot Nash Equilibrium
learning_objective: Use best replies to compute the symmetric pure-strategy Cournot equilibrium.
preliminaries: Use the \(n\)-firm Cournot game with \(BR_i(Q_{-i})=\max\{0,(1-c-Q_{-i})/2\}\), where \(c\in(0,1)\).
question: Find a pure-strategy Nash equilibrium of the Cournot game.
solution: Look for a symmetric equilibrium with \(q_i=q\) for all \(i\). Since \(c\in(0,1)\), the candidate will have positive output. The best-response equation is \[q=\frac{1-c-(n-1)q}{2}.\] Therefore \[(n+1)q=1-c,\qquad q^*=\frac{1-c}{n+1}.\] This quantity is positive and gives total output \(n(1-c)/(n+1)<1-c<1\), so the positive-price branch is valid. Substituting \(Q_{-i}=(n-1)q^*\) into the best reply gives \[BR_i(Q_{-i})=\frac{1-c-(n-1)(1-c)/(n+1)}{2}=\frac{1-c}{n+1}=q^*.\] Thus \((q^*,\ldots,q^*)\) is a pure-strategy Nash equilibrium.

source.original_question: For the same n-firm Cournot market with linear demand and constant marginal cost, find a pure-strategy Nash equilibrium.
source.notes: Converted from the source solution and independently checked by substitution into the best-response function.

validation.notes: The symmetric quantity is positive and is exactly each firm's best response to rivals' quantities.

title: Uniqueness of Pure Cournot Equilibrium
learning_objective: Prove that the pure-strategy Cournot equilibrium is unique for two or more firms.
preliminaries: In the Cournot game, \(BR_i(Q_{-i})=\max\{0,(1-c-Q_{-i})/2\}\), with \(c\in(0,1)\).
question: Is the pure-strategy Nash equilibrium unique for \(n=2\)? Is it unique for \(n\ge 3\)?
solution: The pure-strategy equilibrium is unique for every \(n\ge 2\). First, no firm can produce zero in equilibrium. If firm \(i\) produced zero while some active set of \(m\le n-1\) other firms produced total output \(Q_{-i}<1-c\), then firm \(i\)'s best response would be positive. If instead \(Q_{-i}\ge 1-c\), active firms would earn nonpositive profit and could weakly improve by reducing output; at least one active firm facing large rival output would not satisfy the positive best-response condition. More directly, for every active firm \(i\), the first-order condition is \[q_i=1-c-Q,\] where \(Q=\sum_j q_j\). Hence all active firms have the same output. If \(m\) firms are active, each active output is \((1-c)/(m+1)\), and total active output is \(m(1-c)/(m+1)<1-c\). Any inactive firm would then have positive best response \((1-c-Q)/2>0\), a contradiction unless \(m=n\). Therefore all firms are active and symmetric, so \[q_i=\frac{1-c}{n+1}\quad\text{for every }i.\] This is the equilibrium already verified, so no other pure-strategy Nash equilibrium exists for \(n=2\) or for \(n\ge 3\).

source.original_question: In the same Cournot game, ask whether pure-strategy Nash equilibrium is unique for n=2 and for n>=3.
source.notes: Converted from the source solution and strengthened with an active-set proof of uniqueness.

validation.notes: The active-set argument rules out asymmetric and inactive-firm equilibria.

title: Project Choice with Uncertain Beliefs
learning_objective: Choose between risky projects when the probability distribution is itself uncertain.
preliminaries: There are two states, \(H\) and \(L\). Project I pays \(2\) in state \(H\) and \(5\) in state \(L\). Project II pays \(5\) in state \(H\) and \(3\) in state \(L\). Bernoulli utility is \(u(x)=\log x\). With probability \(1/2\), the state distribution is \(p=(1/3,2/3)\); with probability \(1/2\), it is \(q=(2/3,1/3)\).
question: Without additional information, which project should the individual choose?
solution: The unconditional probability of state \(H\) is \((1/2)(1/3)+(1/2)(2/3)=1/2\), and the unconditional probability of state \(L\) is also \(1/2\). Therefore expected utility from Project I is \[EU_I=\frac12\log 2+\frac12\log 5=\log\sqrt{10}.\] Expected utility from Project II is \[EU_{II}=\frac12\log 5+\frac12\log 3=\log\sqrt{15}.\] Since \(15>10\), \(\log\sqrt{15}>\log\sqrt{10}\). The individual should choose Project II.

source.original_question: An expected-utility maximizer with log utility must choose one of two projects. Project I pays 2 in H and 5 in L; Project II pays 5 in H and 3 in L. The distribution is p=(1/3,2/3) or q=(2/3,1/3), each with probability 1/2.
source.notes: Converted from the source solution and independently checked by reducing the mixture to unconditional state probabilities.

validation.notes: The expected utilities simplify to \(\log\sqrt{10}\) and \(\log\sqrt{15}\), so Project II is optimal.

title: Value of Learning the Probability Distribution
learning_objective: Compute the expected utility gain from observing which prior distribution governs payoffs before choosing a project.
preliminaries: Use the two-project problem with log utility. Before choosing the project, the individual can learn whether the true state distribution is \(p=(1/3,2/3)\) or \(q=(2/3,1/3)\). Each distribution is equally likely ex ante.
question: Compute the increase in expected utility from being told whether the state distribution is \(p\) or \(q\) before choosing a project.
solution: Under distribution \(p\), expected utilities are \[EU_I(p)=\frac13\log2+\frac23\log5=\frac13\log50,\] and \[EU_{II}(p)=\frac13\log5+\frac23\log3=\frac13\log45.\] Since \(50>45\), choose Project I under \(p\). Under distribution \(q\), \[EU_I(q)=\frac23\log2+\frac13\log5=\frac13\log20,\] and \[EU_{II}(q)=\frac23\log5+\frac13\log3=\frac13\log75.\] Since \(75>20\), choose Project II under \(q\). Expected utility with information is \[\frac12\cdot\frac13\log50+\frac12\cdot\frac13\log75=\frac16\log(3750).\] Without information, the individual chooses Project II and gets \(\log\sqrt{15}=\frac12\log15\). The gain is \[\frac16\log(3750)-\frac12\log15=\frac16\log\left(\frac{3750}{15^3}\right)=\frac16\log\left(\frac{10}{9}\right).\]

source.original_question: Compute the increase in expected utility if the individual is told whether the true distribution is p or q before choosing Project I or Project II.
source.notes: Converted from the source solution and independently checked by comparing project values under each distribution.

validation.notes: The state-contingent optimal projects and utility gain \((1/6)\log(10/9)\) are correct.

title: A Noisy Signal with Zero Decision Value
learning_objective: Use posterior predictive probabilities to determine whether a signal changes the optimal action.
preliminaries: Instead of learning whether the distribution is \(p\) or \(q\), the individual observes one draw from the true distribution before choosing a project. The payoff state is then determined by a second independent draw from the same true distribution.
question: How much would the individual pay to observe one draw from the true distribution before choosing a project?
solution: The ex ante probability of observing \(H\) is \(1/2\). By Bayes' rule, after signal \(H\), \[\Pr(p\mid H)=\frac{(1/2)(1/3)}{(1/2)(1/3)+(1/2)(2/3)}=\frac13,\qquad \Pr(q\mid H)=\frac23.\] The posterior predictive probability of \(H\) on the payoff draw is \((1/3)(1/3)+(2/3)(2/3)=5/9\). After signal \(L\), the posterior weights are \(\Pr(p\mid L)=2/3\) and \(\Pr(q\mid L)=1/3\), so the posterior predictive probability of \(H\) is \((2/3)(1/3)+(1/3)(2/3)=4/9\). For any predictive probability \(\pi\) of state \(H\), Project II is preferred to Project I when \[\pi\log5+(1-\pi)\log3\ge \pi\log2+(1-\pi)\log5.\] This is equivalent to \[\pi\log(5/2)+(1-\pi)\log(3/5)\ge 0,\] whose cutoff is below \(4/9\). Therefore Project II is optimal after both possible signals. Since the signal never changes the chosen project, it has zero value in expected utility terms. The individual would pay \(0\) for this signal.

source.original_question: A less precise information source gives one draw from the actual distribution before the project choice, and the payoff is determined by a second independent draw. How much would the individual pay to observe the draw?
source.notes: Converted from the source answer and independently checked using posterior predictive probabilities.

validation.notes: Bayesian updating gives predictive probabilities \(5/9\) and \(4/9\); Project II remains optimal after both signals, so the value is zero.

title: Income Elasticity from a Multiplicatively Separable Expenditure Function
learning_objective: Derive Walrasian demand and income elasticity from an expenditure function of the form \(e(p,u)=f(u)g(p)\).
preliminaries: An individual has expenditure function \(e(p,u)=f(u)g(p)\), where \(p\in\mathbb{R}_{++}^n\), \(f\) is strictly increasing and twice continuously differentiable, and \(g\) is twice continuously differentiable. Assume differentiability conditions for Roy's identity and that good \(i\)'s demand is positive.
question: Compute the income elasticity of Walrasian demand for an arbitrary good \(i\).
solution: The indirect utility \(V(p,w)\) solves \(e(p,V(p,w))=w\), so \[f(V(p,w))g(p)=w,\qquad V(p,w)=f^{-1}\left(\frac{w}{g(p)}\right).\] Roy's identity gives \[x_i(p,w)=-\frac{\partial V(p,w)/\partial p_i}{\partial V(p,w)/\partial w}.\] Differentiating, the common factor \((f^{-1})'(w/g(p))\) cancels, yielding \[x_i(p,w)=\frac{w}{g(p)}\frac{\partial g(p)}{\partial p_i}\] up to the sign convention embedded in the expenditure derivative. For a valid expenditure function, Shephard's lemma implies the resulting demand is nonnegative; equivalently write \(x_i(p,w)=w\,a_i(p)\), where \(a_i(p)\) depends only on prices. Therefore \[\frac{\partial x_i}{\partial w}\frac{w}{x_i}=a_i(p)\frac{w}{w a_i(p)}=1.\] The income elasticity of every positively demanded good is \(1\).

source.original_question: An individual has expenditure function e(p,u)=f(u)g(p), with f strictly increasing and f,g twice continuously differentiable. Compute the income elasticity of Walrasian demand for an arbitrary good i.
source.notes: Converted from the source solution and independently checked by inverting expenditure and applying Roy's identity.

validation.notes: Demand is homogeneous of degree one in wealth, so income elasticity is one for positive demand.

title: Utility Representations Need Not All Be Homogeneous
learning_objective: Distinguish a property of preferences from a property of every utility representation.
preliminaries: Preferences are represented by some utility function. Any strictly increasing transformation of a utility representation represents the same preferences.
question: True or false: any utility function representing preferences whose expenditure function is \(e(p,u)=f(u)g(p)\) must be homogeneous of degree one. Explain.
solution: False. Homogeneity of a particular utility index is not ordinally invariant. If \(u(x)\) represents the preferences and is homogeneous of degree one, then \(\tilde u(x)=[u(x)]^2\) also represents the same preferences whenever \(u(x)>0\), because the square function is strictly increasing on positive utility levels. But \[\tilde u(\lambda x)=[u(\lambda x)]^2=[\lambda u(x)]^2=\lambda^2\tilde u(x),\] so \(\tilde u\) is homogeneous of degree two, not degree one. More generally, strictly increasing transformations preserve preferences while changing cardinal homogeneity properties. Thus it cannot be true that every utility representation must be homogeneous of degree one.

source.original_question: For preferences with expenditure function e(p,u)=f(u)g(p), decide whether any utility function representing the preferences must be homogeneous of degree one.
source.notes: Converted from the source answer and independently checked using monotone transformations.

validation.notes: A monotone transformation changes the degree of homogeneity while preserving preferences.
